items.name: Vessel general
items.definition: General functions related to vessels.
items.commonDefinition: General topics related to vessels.

items.name: Administration
items.definition: Administrative and general functions related to vessel services.
items.commonDefinition: Administrative and general topics related to vessel services.

items.name: Vessel identification
items.definition:
Identification of the vessel by e.g. name, IMO no. or the recognised organisation's id.no.
Characterisation of vessel by e.g. purpose or shape.
items.commonDefinition:
Identification of the vessel by e.g. name, IMO no. or the recognised organisation's id.no.
Characterisation of vessel by e.g. purpose or shape.

items.name: Vessel construction - equipment supplying
items.definition: Supplying products to the vessel's builder during construction of the vessel.
items.commonDefinition: The process of supplying products to the vessel's builder during construction of the vessel.

items.name: Authorisations reception
items.definition:
Reception of authorisations from Flags to perform specified services for a vessel. The authorisation may be specific for the vessel, or general and applicable to the vessel.
Authorisations may cover:
- design assessment
- survey
- certificate issuance.
items.commonDefinition:
Authorisations received from Flags to perform specified services for a vessel. The authorisation may be specific for the vessel, or general and applicable to the vessel.
Authorisations may cover:
- design assessment
- survey
- certificate issuance.

items.name: Administrative and operational instructions - I, other
items.commonName: Administrative and operational instructions - I, other

items.name: Vessel servicing
items.definition: Services performed for a vessel, e.g. as defined in a contract for classification or product certification.

items.name: Vessel servicing - communication
items.definition: Communication including meetings with external or internal parties, e.g. customers or authorities, related to vessel services.

items.name: Vessel servicing formalisation
items.definition: Formalisation of services performed for a vessel.

items.name: Vessel servicing - quotation request reception
items.definition: Reception of requests for quotations for vessel related services from external or internal parties.

items.name: Vessel servicing - quotation submitting
items.definition: Submitting of quotations for vessel related services to external or internal parties.

items.name: Vessel servicing - service request reception
items.definition: Reception of requests for vessel related services from external or internal parties.

items.name: Vessel servicing - service agreements
items.definition: Agreements with external parties for vessel related services.

items.name: Vessel servicing - change order reception
items.definition: Reception of change orders to agreements for vessel related services from external or internal parties.

items.name: Vessel servicing - agreements with other classification societies
items.definition: Agreements with other classification societies relating to services for the vessel. The agreements may be specific for the vessel, or general and applicable to the vessel.

items.name: Vessel servicing - execution
items.definition: Execution of services related to a vessel.

items.name: Vessel servicing - surveys and audits execution
items.definition: Execution of surveys and audits on a vessel.

items.name: Vessel servicing - jobs execution
items.definition: Execution of jobs for a vessel.

items.name: Economy
items.definition: Economical aspects for vessel related services.

items.name: Pricing
items.definition: Pricing of vessel related services.

items.name: Budgeting
items.definition: Budgeting of vessel related services.

items.name: Accounting
items.definition: Accounting for vessel related services.

items.name: External invoicing
items.definition: Submitting and receiving invoices for vessel related services to or from external parties.

items.name: External invoicing - submitting
items.definition: Submitting of invoices for vessel related services to external parties.

items.name: External invoicing - receiving
items.definition: Receiving invoices for vessel related services from external parties.

items.name: Internal invoicing
items.definition: Submitting and receiving invoices for vessel related services to or from internal parties.

items.name: Internal invoicing - submitting
items.definition: Submitting of invoices for vessel related services to internal parties.

items.name: Internal invoicing - receiving
items.definition: Receiving invoices for vessel related services from internal parties.

items.name: Requirements definition
items.definition: Defining technical and procedural requirements for a vessel.

items.name: Class rules compliance
items.definition:
The class rules that the vessel shall comply with, e.g.:
- regime (HSLC, Offshore, Ship)
- edition
- parts and chapters.

items.name: Statutory regulations compliance
items.definition:
The statutory regulations that the vessel shall comply with, e.g.:
- regulation names
- editions.

items.name: National regulations compliance
items.definition:
The national regulations that the vessel shall comply with, e.g.:
- regulation names
- editions.

items.name: Standards compliance
items.definition:
The standards that the vessel shall comply with, e.g.:
- standards names
- editions.

items.name: Documentation requiring
items.definition:
Requiring documentation for:
- design assessment of vessel and products intended to be installed on board the vessel
- verification of construction of vessel and products intended to be installed on board the vessel
- qualification of builder and manufacturers of products intended to be installed on board the vessel
- operational support of vessel and products intended to be installed on board the vessel.

items.name: Documentation requiring for vessels
items.definition:
Requiring documentation for:
- design assessment of vessel
- verification of construction of vessel
- qualification of builder
- operational support of vessel.

items.name: Documentation requiring for products
items.definition:
Requiring documentation for:
- design assessment of products intended to be installed on board the vessel
- verification of construction of products intended to be installed on board the vessel
- qualification of manufacturers of products intended to be installed on board the vessel
- operational support of products intended to be installed on board the vessel.

items.name: Documentation requiring for operational support of vessel
items.definition: Requiring documentation for operational support of vessel.
items.commonDefinition: Documentation required to be carried on board during operation.

items.name: Compliance document requiring
items.definition: Requiring compliance documents for products intended to be installed on board the vessel.

items.name: Surveyor tasks requiring
items.definition: Tasks required to be performed by the recognised organisation's surveyors, targeting the vessel and products intended to be installed on board the vessel, during construction and operation.

items.name: Surveyor tasks requiring, vessel under construction
items.definition: Tasks required to be performed by the recognised organisation's surveyors, targeting the vessel during construction.

items.name: Surveyor tasks requiring, products intended to be installed on board the vessel
items.commonName: Survey requirements, products intended to be installed on board
items.definition: Tasks required to be performed by the recognised organisation's surveyors, targeting products intended to be installed on board the vessel, during construction.

items.name: Surveyor tasks requiring, vessel in operation
items.definition: Tasks required to be performed by the recognised organisation's surveyors, targeting the vessel and products installed on board the vessel, during operation.

items.name: Survey arrangements
items.definition:
The survey arrangements that apply to the vessel, for service Fleet in service (FiS).
Survey arrangements are alternatives to periodical surveys for main class.

items.name: Survey arrangement planned maintenance system
items.definition: A survey arrangement based on audits of an approved and implemented planned maintenance system on board covering all items in the machinery or drilling list.
items.commonDefinition:
A processing system running a planned maintenance system (PMS).
The PMS may cover e.g. machinery or drilling.

items.name: Survey arrangement condition based maintenance
items.definition: A survey arrangement based on audits of an approved and implemented condition based maintenance system. Applicable for selected machinery items which are part of the machinery or drilling list. Maintenance tasks are established and planned based on one or more condition monitoring methods, diagnostics and prognostics
items.commonDefinition: A survey arrangement based on audits of an approved and implemented condition based maintenance system. Applicable for selected machinery items which are part of the machinery or drilling list. Maintenance tasks are established and planned based on one or more condition monitoring methods, diagnostics and prognostics

items.name: Survey arrangement thrusters condition monitoring
items.definition: Condition monitoring of the thrusters.
items.commonDefinition: A survey arrangement based on audits of an approved and implemented condition monitoring program on board. The arrangement allows the manager to adjust the maintenance intervals based on condition monitoring of the thrusters, e.g. by means of vibration and oil analyses. Such monitoring may be accepted as an alternative to periodical survey of the thrusters.

items.name: Survey arrangement hull planned maintenance
items.definition: A survey arrangement based on audits of an approved and implemented planned maintenance system on board covering all items in the hull inventory list.

items.name: Survey arrangement planned maintenance, reliability centred
items.definition: A survey arrangement based on audits of an approved and implemented planned maintenance system based on reliability centred maintenance (RCM) analyses.

items.name: Survey arrangement machinery bearing wear monitoring
items.definition: A survey arrangement based on audits of an approved and implemented condition monitoring program on board that relates to the machinery bearings.
items.commonDefinition: A survey arrangement based on audits of an approved and implemented condition monitoring program on board that relates to the machinery bearings.

items.name: Survey arrangement shared structural inspection program
items.definition: A survey arrangement based on audits of an approved and implemented shared structural inspection (SSI) program allowing up to half of the structure, covered by the unit's IIP (In-service inspection plan) structural inspection programme, to be inspected by owner's qualified personnel.
items.commonDefinition: A survey arrangement based on audits of an approved and implemented shared structural inspection program on board.

items.name: Planning and reporting
items.definition: Planning and reporting of vessel related services.

items.name: Documentation scheduling
items.definition:
Scheduling of documentation that shall be submitted to and from external and internal parties, e.g.:
- documents for plan approval
- documentation of approval process (approval letters).

items.name: Approval planning
items.definition: Planning of the approval activities.

items.name: Approval reporting
items.definition:
Reporting of the approval activities, e.g.:
- approval status related to documentation requirements
- approval status related to customer documents

items.name: Fabrication scheduling
items.definition: Scheduling of the fabrication of the vessel and products intended to be installed on board the vessel.

items.name: Fabrication survey planning
items.definition: Planning of the survey activities relating to fabrication of the vessel and products intended to be installed on board the vessel.

items.name: Fabrication survey reporting
items.definition: Reporting of the survey activities relating to fabrication of the vessel and products intended to be installed on board the vessel.

items.name: Operational survey planning
items.definition: Planning of operational surveys of the vessel.

items.name: Operational survey reporting
items.definition: Reporting of operational surveys of the vessel.

items.name: Planning and reporting, other services
items.definition: Planning and reporting of other vessel related services than those described in 041 to 048.

items.name: Compliance documents issuing and renewing
items.definition:
Issuing and renewing of compliance documents relating to the:
- vessel
- products installed or intended to be installed on board the vessel
- vessel's owner or operator
- personnel working on the vessel.

items.name: Vessel compliance documents issuing and renewing
items.definition: Issuing and renewing of the vessel's compliance documents.

items.name: Classification certificates issuing and renewing
items.definition: Issuing and renewing of the vessel's classification certificate.

items.name: Statutory certificates issuing and renewing
items.definition: Issuing and renewing of the vessel's statutory certificates.

items.name: Statements of compliance issuing and renewing
items.definition: Issuing and renewing of the vessel's statements of compliance

items.name: Company compliance documents issuing and renewing
items.definition: Issuing and renewing of company compliance documents, for companies controlling or delivering services to the vessel.

items.name: Personnel compliance documents issuing and renewing
items.definition: Issuing and renewing of personnel compliance documents, for personnel delivering services to the vessel.

items.name: Product compliance documents issuing and renewing
items.definition: Issuing and renewing of compliance documents relating to products installed or intended to be installed on board the vessel.

items.name: Quality assurance
items.definition: Quality assurance, or QA for short, is the systematic monitoring and evaluation of the various aspects of a vessel related project, service or facility to maximize the probability that minimum standards of quality are being attained by the production process. QA cannot absolutely guarantee the production of quality products. Two principles included in QA are: "Fit for purpose" - the product should be suitable for the intended purpose; and "Right first time" - mistakes should be eliminated. QA includes regulation of the quality of raw materials, products, services related to production, and management, production and inspection processes.

items.name: Quality standards application
items.definition: Application of standards, e.g. ISO 9001, in order to perform quality assurance upon services related to the vessel.

items.name: Recognised organisation internal audits
items.definition: Auditing of the recognised organisation's organisational units delivering services to the vessel, e.g. the site team, performed by other units within the recognised organisation.

items.name: Auditing performed upon the recognised organisation
items.commonName: Audits performed upon the recognised organisation
items.definition: Auditing of the recognised organisation's organisational units delivering services to the vessels, e.g. the site team, performed by external parties.

items.name: Auditing performed by the recognised organisation
items.commonName: Audits performed by the recognised organisation
items.definition: Auditing of external parties delivering services to the vessel, performed by the recognised organisation.

items.name: Assessment of builders
items.definition: Quality assessment of yards building, modifying or repairing the vessel, or parts of the vessel.

items.name: Assessment of subcontractors
items.definition: Quality assessment of subcontractors (e.g. manufacturers) to yards building, modifying or repairing the vessel, or parts of the vessel.

items.name: Complaints handling
items.definition: Handling of complaints from external or internal parties related to the recognised organisation's services to the vessel.

items.name: Technical information
items.definition: General technical information related to the vessel.

items.name: Vessel specification
items.definition: Overall technical specification of the vessel and its equipment.

items.name: Inventory compilation
items.definition: An overview of the physical items (hull and equipment) on board the vessel. The inventory may include information about the materials used in the items.

items.name: Manning and passengers carriage
items.definition:
Requirements and limitations to:
- manning of the vessel, i.e. the master and the crew required for navigation, engineering and maintenance of the vessel or engaged to provide services for the persons carried on board
- carriage of special personnel, i.e. persons who are needed for the particular operational duties of the vessel
- passengers, i.e. every person other than the master and the members of the crew or other persons employed or engaged in any capacity on board a vessel on the business of that vessel, excepting children under one year of age.

items.name: Commissioning
items.definition: Commissioning is the process of assuring that all systems and products on the vessel are installed and tested according to contractual requirements between the builder and the owner. The main objective of commissioning is to effect the safe and orderly handover of the vessel from the builder to the owner, guaranteeing its operability in terms of performance, reliability, safety and information traceability. Additionally, when executed in a planned and effective way, commissioning normally represents an essential factor for the fulfilment of schedule, costs, safety and quality requirements of the project.

items.name: Sea trial
items.definition:
A sea trial is the testing phase of a vessel. It is usually the last phase of construction and takes place on open water, and can last from a few hours to many days. Sea trials are conducted to measure a vessel’s performance and general seaworthiness. Testing of a vessel’s speed, manoeuvrability, equipment and safety features are usually conducted. Usually in attendance are technical representatives from the builder, governing and certification officials, and representatives of the owners. Successful sea trials subsequently lead to a vessel’s certification for commissioning and acceptance by its owner.
Sea trials can also refer to a short test trip undertaken by a prospective buyer of a new or used vessel as one determining factor in whether to purchase the vessel.

items.name: Signboards placement
items.definition: Signboards are any kind of visual graphics created to display information to a persons on board the vessel. Rules and regulations set up requirements to placements of signboards providing safety or operational related messages.

items.name: Spare parts carrying
items.definition:
Carrying spare parts for on board equipment.
Lists of recommended spare parts may be found in the class rules.

items.name: Decommissioning
items.definition: The process for removing the vessel from operation, scrapping.

items.name: Vessel management
items.definition:
Vessel management in the operational phase, e.g.:
- operation
- maintenance
- inspection
- safety management
- security management.

items.name: Vessel operating
items.definition: Vessel in-service operation.

items.name: Voyage describing
items.definition: Describing voyages performed by the vessel.
items.commonDefinition: Data describing voyages performed by the vessel.

items.name: Loading describing
items.definition: Describing the loading condition of the vessel, e.g. draft.
items.commonDefinition: Data describing the loading condition of the vessel, e.g. draft.

items.name: Maintenance
items.definition:
Vessel maintenance performed by the crew, service suppliers or yards, covering e.g.:
- ad-hoc maintenance
- planned maintenance
- repairs.

items.name: Repairing
items.definition: Repairing the vessel or its equipment.

items.name: Logging
items.definition: Logging of events related to the operation of the vessel.

items.name: Inspection planning
items.definition:
Planning of inspections performed upon the vessel and its equipment, performed by e.g.
- crew
- classification society
- flag, port or coastal state authorities
- charterers.

items.name: Inspection reporting
items.definition:
Reporting of inspections performed upon the vessel and its equipment, performed by e.g.
- crew
- classification society
- flag, port or coastal state authorities
- charterers.

items.name: Safety management
items.definition: A structured and documented system enabling the Company (owner / manager) of the vessel to implement effectively the Company's safety and environmental protection policy.

items.definition:
Occurrence of events related to the vessel, covering accidents and dangerous situations, e.g.:
- fatalities
- fire
- flooding
- grounding
- loss of steering
- loss of propulsion.

items.name: Cargo handling general

items.definition: Describing the vessel's cargo.

items.name: Cargo handling
items.definition: Cargo loading, unloading, storing, treatment and securing.
items.commonDefinition:
Arrangements for cargo loading, unloading, storing, treatment and securing.
Cargo (or freight) refers to goods or produce transported by the vessel, generally for commercial gain.

items.name: Cargo handling equipment containment
items.definition:
Spaces for handling, control and monitoring of cargo, cargo loading and unloading, and cargo treatment, and for storage of cargo gear.
The spaces may also be used for other purposes.
items.commonDefinition:
Spaces for handling, control and monitoring of cargo, cargo loading and unloading, and cargo treatment, and for storage of cargo gear.
The spaces may also be used for other purposes.

items.name: Cargo handling control equipment containment
items.definition: Cargo handling control equipment containment
items.commonDefinition:
Rooms for
- control and monitoring of cargo handling operations during loading and unloading
- control and monitoring of cargo related parameters during voyage.
The rooms may also be used for other purposes.

items.name: Cargo gear containment
items.definition: Cargo gear containment
items.commonDefinition:
Lockers for storage of cargo gear, e.g. loose cargo securing devices, hoses, pipe reducers, pipe connection devices, spool pieces.
The lockers may also be used for other purposes.

items.name: Structural materials use
items.definition:
Use of materials for the construction of the main vessel structure.
Use of materials for constructions of secondary structures (e.g. drillfloor), and for products (e.g. hatches, pipes, machinery) is covered by the functions related to such items.
items.commonDefinition:
Materials used for the construction of the main vessel structure.
Materials used for constructions of secondary structures (e.g. drillfloor), and for products (e.g. hatches, pipes, machinery) is covered by the functions related to such items.

items.name: Cargo evacuation
items.definition:
Pumping of cargo for evacuation of cargo tanks.
Evacuation means removal of the remaining cargo that the main cargo pumps are not able to handle, by means of pumps that create vacuum within the cargo tanks and thereby suck the cargo to wells on the main cargo pumps inlets.
items.commonDefinition:
Pumping units (i.e. pump, pump driver, shaft, and foundation) for evacuation of cargo tanks.
Evacuation means removal of the remaining cargo that the main cargo pumps are not able to handle, by means of pumps that create vacuum within the cargo tanks and thereby suck the cargo to wells on the main cargo pumps inlets.

items.name: Cargo evacuation - pumping unit
items.definition:
Pumping of cargo for evacuation of cargo tanks, by an individual pumping unit.
Evacuation means removal (unloading, stripping) of the remaining cargo that the main cargo pumps are not able to handle, by means of pumps that create vacuum within the cargo tanks and thereby suck the cargo to wells on the main cargo pumps inlets.
items.commonDefinition:
A pumping unit (i.e. pump, pump driver, shaft, and foundation) for evacuation of cargo tanks.
Evacuation means removal (unloading, stripping) of the remaining cargo that the main cargo pumps are not able to handle, by means of pumps that create vacuum within the cargo tanks and thereby suck the cargo to wells on the main cargo pumps inlets.

items.name: Cargo stripping
items.definition:
Stripping of cargo.
Stripping means removal of the remaining cargo that the main cargo pumps are not able to handle, by means of pumps or ejectors that suck more efficient or that are connected to a lower point within the cargo tanks than the main cargo pumps.
items.commonDefinition:
Pumping units (i.e. pump, pump driver, shaft, and foundation) or ejectors for stripping of cargo.
Stripping means removal of the remaining cargo that the main cargo pumps are not able to handle, by means of pumps or ejectors that suck more efficient or that are connected to a lower point within the cargo tanks than the main cargo pumps.

items.name: Cargo stripping - pumping unit
items.definition:
Stripping of cargo, by an individual pumping unit or ejector.
Stripping means removal of the remaining cargo that the main cargo pumps are not able to handle, by means of pumps or ejectors that suck more efficient or that are connected to a lower point within the cargo tanks than the main cargo pumps.
items.commonDefinition:
A pumping unit (i.e. pump, pump driver, shaft, and foundation) or ejector for stripping of cargo.
Stripping means removal of the remaining cargo that the main cargo pumps are not able to handle, by means of pumps or ejectors that suck more efficient or that are connected to a lower point within the cargo tanks than the main cargo pumps.

items.name: Cargo vapourizing
items.definition:
Vaporizing or heating of the cargo with the purpose of:
- filling the tank space with gas during unloading
- production of gas for fuel
- use of gas for 'gassing up', i.e. replacing the inert gas with cargo vapour before loading

items.name: Cargo vapourizer
items.definition: Vaporizing of the cargo by means of an evaporator. The evaporator is normally a heat exchanger using steam, sea water or other heating medium, but may also be an electric heater.

items.name: Vapour handling and recovery
items.definition:
Handling of vapour, i.e. cargo in gaseous form, by
- return of vapour to shore,
- supply of boil-off gas to combustion installations on board
- supply of boil-off gas to a reliquefaction plant on board
- supply of vapour to a recovery plant on board

Systems for handling of vapour, i.e. cargo in gaseous form, by

- supply of boil-off gas to a reliquefaction plant on board

items.name: Vapour handling
items.definition:
Vapour handling with or without compressor assistance, i.e.:
- return of vapour to shore,
- supply of boil-off gas to combustion installations on board
- supply of boil-off gas to a reliquefaction plant on board.

items.name: Corrosion prevention by sacrificial anodes
items.definition: Control of the corrosion of a structure by attaching metal rods to the surface of the structure, thereby making the rods and the structure to the anodes and cathode of electrochemical cells.
items.commonDefinition: Metal rods attached to the surface of a metal structure, used to control the corrosion of the structure by making the rods and the structure to the anodes and cathode of electrochemical cells.

items.definition: Impressing a direct current (DC) between anodes and the structure, providing corrosion prevention for the structure.
items.commonDefinition: A direct current (DC) power system impressing a current between anodes and the structure, providing corrosion prevention for the structure.

items.name: Corrosion prevention monitoring
items.definition: Monitoring the effect of the corrosion prevention.
items.commonDefinition: A system monitoring the effect of the corrosion prevention.

items.name: Cargo storing
items.definition:
Storing of cargo in holds, tanks, spaces and on decks, and
- control of the chemical composition or the cargo compartment atmosphere
- pressure equalisation between the cargo compartments and the external atmosphere
- cleaning of the cargo compartments
- control and monitoring of the cargo and the cargo compartments.

items.name: Cargo containment in independent tanks
items.definition: Cargo containment in independent tank arrangements.
items.commonDefinition:
Independent tank arrangements for containment of cargo. Independent implies that the tanks are self-supporting, do not form part of the vessel's main structure and are not essential to the strength if the vessel's main structure.
Each arrangement may include:
- an independent tank
- supports for the independent tank
- a hold space surrounding the independent tank
- supporting structures accommodating loads and moments from the independent tank.

items.name: Cargo containment in an independent tank
items.definition: Cargo containment in an independent tank.
items.commonDefinition: An independent tank for containment of cargo.

items.definition: Containment of an independent cargo tank.
items.commonDefinition: A space surrounding an independent cargo tank.

items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the independent cargo tank.
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the independent cargo tank.

items.name: Cargo containment arrangement for an independent tank
items.definition: Cargo containment in an independent tank arrangement.
items.commonDefinition:
An independent tank arrangement for containment of cargo. Each arrangement may include:
- an independent tank
- supports for the independent tank
- a hold space surrounding the independent tank
- supporting structures accommodating loads and moments from the independent tank.

items.name: Cargo containment in independent tanks type A
items.commonName: Cargo independent tank arrangements type A
items.definition: Cargo containment in independent tank arrangements of type A.
items.commonDefinition:
Arrangements for cargo containment in independent tanks of type A. Independent implies that the tanks are self-supporting, do not form part of the vessel's main structure and are not essential to the strength if the vessel's main structure.
Type A implies that the tanks are designed primarily using classical ship-structural analysis procedures.
Each arrangement may include:
- an independent tank, the tank boundaries are the primary barriers of the cargo containment system
- thermal insulation
- supports for the independent tank
- secondary barriers for the cargo containment system
- a hold space surrounding the independent tank
- supporting structures accommodating loads and moments from the independent tank.

items.name: Cargo containment in an independent tank type A
items.definition: Cargo containment in an independent tank type A.
items.commonDefinition: An independent tank type A for cargo containment.

items.name: Cargo independent tank type A thermal insulating
items.commonName: Cargo independent tank type A thermal insulation
items.definition: Thermal insulating of an independent tank of type A.
items.commonDefinition: Thermal insulation for an independent tank of type A.

items.name: Cargo containment in an independent tank type A - tank supports
items.commonName: Cargo independent tank type A supports
items.definition: Transfer of loads and moments from an independent tank of type A to the vessel's main structure, i.e. the inner hull.
items.commonDefinition:
Supports for transfer of loads and moments from an independent tank of type A to the vessel's main structure, i.e. the inner hull.
The tank should be supported in a manner which will prevent bodily movement of the tank under static and dynamic loads while allowing contraction and expansion of the tank under temperature variations and hull deflections without due stressing of the tank and of the hull.

items.name: Cargo containment in an independent tank type A - secondary barrier
items.commonName: Cargo independent tank type A secondary barrier
items.definition: Temporary containment of any envisaged leakage of liquid through the primary barrier and preventing the lowering of the temperature of the vessel's structure to an unsafe level.
items.commonDefinition: A liquid-resisting outer element of a cargo tank of type A designed to afford temporary containment of any envisaged leakage of liquid through the primary barrier and to prevent the lowering of the temperature of the vessel's structure to an unsafe level.

items.name: Cargo containment in an independent tank type A - hold space
items.commonName: Cargo independent tank type A, hold space
items.definition: Containment of an independent cargo tank type A.
items.commonDefinition: A space enclosed by the vessel's structure in which an independent cargo tank of type A is situated, i.e. a part of the inner hull.

items.name: Cargo containment in an independent tank type A - tank supporting structure
items.commonName: Cargo independent tank type A supporting structure
items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure in order to accommodate loads and moments from the independent cargo tank type A.
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for strengthening of the main vessel structure in order to accommodate loads and moments from the independent cargo tank of type A.

items.name: Cargo containment in an independent tank arrangement type A
items.commonName: Cargo independent tank arrangement type A
items.definition: Cargo containment in an independent tank arrangement of type A.
items.commonDefinition: An arrangement for cargo containment in an independent tank of type A.

items.name: Cargo containment in independent tanks type B
items.commonName: Cargo independent tank arrangements type B
items.definition:
Cargo containment in independent tank arrangements of type B.
Independent implies that the tanks are self-supporting, do not form part of the vessel's main structure and are not essential to the strength if the vessel's main structure.
Type B implies that the tanks are designed using model tests, refined analytical tools and analysis methods to determine stress levels, fatigue life and crack propagation characteristics.
items.commonDefinition:
Arrangements for cargo containment in independent tanks of type B. Independent implies that the tanks are self-supporting, do not form part of the vessel's main structure and are not essential to the strength if the vessel's main structure.
Type B implies that the tanks are designed using model tests, refined analytical tools and analysis methods to determine stress levels, fatigue life and crack propagation characteristics.
Each arrangement may include:
- an independent tank, the tank boundaries are the primary barriers of the cargo containment system
- thermal insulation
- supports for the independent tank
- drip trays
- a hold space surrounding the independent tank
- supporting structures accommodating loads and moments from the independent tank.

items.name: Cargo containment in an independent tank type B
items.definition: Cargo containment in an independent tank type B.
items.commonDefinition: An independent tank type B for cargo containment.

items.name: Cargo independent tank type B thermal insulating
items.commonName: Cargo independent tank type B thermal insulation
items.definition: Thermal insulating of an independent tank of type B.
items.commonDefinition: Thermal insulation for an independent tank of type B.

items.name: Cargo containment in an independent tank type B - tank foundation
items.commonName: Cargo independent tank type B foundation
items.definition: Transfer of loads and moments from an independent tank of type B to the vessel's main structure. The tank should be supported in a manner which will prevent bodily movement of the tank under static and dynamic loads while allowing contraction and expansion of the tank under temperature variations and hull deflections without due stressing of the tank and of the hull.
items.commonDefinition: Supports for transfer of loads and moments from an independent tank of type B to the vessel's main structure. items.commonDefinition:
Arrangements for Cargo gas containment in generic independent tanks. Generic implies that the tank is of an alternative and novel design other than type A, B, C, membrane, semi-membrane or compressed gas. Independent implies that the tanks are self-supporting, do not form part of the vessel's main structure and are not essential to the strength if the vessel's main structure.
Type A implies that the tanks are designed primarily using classical ship-structural analysis procedures.

- an independent tank, the tank boundaries are the primary barriers of the Cargo gas containment system

- supports for the independent tank

items.definition: Treatment of the cargo, e.g. refrigeration, heating, drying, humidification and reliquefaction.

items.name: Cargo refrigeration
items.definition:
Refrigeration (cooling, freezing) of the cargo.
Refrigeration is the process of removing heat from an enclosed space, or from a substance, and moving it to a place where it is unobjectionable. The primary purpose of refrigeration is lowering the temperature of the enclosed space or substance and then maintaining that lower temperature. The term cooling refers generally to any natural or artificial process by which heat is dissipated. Cold is the absence of heat, hence in order to decrease a temperature, one "removes heat", rather than "adding cold."

items.name: Cargo refrigeration - primary refrigerant circuit
items.definition:
A refrigerant circuit with the purpose of either
- cooling of the cargo
- cooling of a medium containing the cargo (air in holds or refrigerated sea water)
- cooling of a secondary refrigerant medium
and heat transfer to a medium outside the vessel, normally sea water.

items.name: Cargo refrigeration by primary refrigeration circuit

items.definition:
Cooling of either

- a medium containing the cargo (air in holds or refrigerated sea water)
and heat transfer to the primary refrigerant medium.
If a secondary refrigerant circuit is installed, the cargo refrigeration takes place there, i.e. this function shall be removed.

items.name: Cargo refrigeration - secondary refrigerant circuit
items.definition: Cargo refigeration by a secondary circuit
items.commonDefinition:
A secondary refrigerant circuit with the purpose of either
- cooling of the cargo
- cooling of a medium containing the cargo (air in holds or refrigerated sea water)
and heat transfer to the primary refrigerant medium.
For dry cargo, brine (water saturated or nearly saturated with a salts, normally not sodium chloride) is normally used as the secondary refrigerant medium.
For liquid cargo, a glycol is often used.
The secondary refrigerant circuit may be split in parts having different temperature and/or brine concentration.

items.definition: Over pressure prevention of the system receiving the regasified cargo by monitoring the gas delivery pressure and shutting off the gas export by closing valves when the pressure limits are exceeded.
items.commonDefinition: A safety instrumentation system preventing over-pressurization of the system receiving the regasified cargo by monitoring the delivery pressure and shutting down the gas export by closing supply valves when the pressure limits are exceeded.

items.name: Regasification plant television monitoring
items.definition: Surveiling regasification plant and gas export manifold by use of a television monitoring system.
items.commonDefinition: Television system (cameras, signal transfer equipment, monitors etc.) for surveillance of regasification plant and gas export manifold.

items.name: No containment

items.name: Cargo securing by fixed and loose devices
items.definition: Securing of unitized cargo by fixed and loose devices.
items.commonDefinition: Devices for securing unitized cargo.

items.name: Cargo securing by fixed devices
items.definition: Cargo securing by use of devices welded on or into the vessel structure to act as supports for cargo or lashings.
items.commonDefinition:
Devices for cargo securing welded on or into the vessel structure to act as supports for cargo or lashings.
Examples: Container sockets, container flush foundations, cloverleaf foundations, breech base foundations, dovetail foundations, counter bearings, guide fittings, lashing pockets, pressure elements, sliding foundations, welding cones, crinkle bars.

items.name: Cargo securing by loose devices
items.definition: Cargo securing by use of loose devices, i.e. devices that are not integral to the vessel's structure.
items.commonDefinition: Devices for cargo securing that are loose, i.e. not integral to the vessel's structure.

items.name: Container securing verification
items.definition:
Verification of the container stowage and securing arrangements by means of calculation and control of:
- internal forces in containers
- forces in securing equipment
- forces in supports.
items.commonDefinition: A computer based system for calculation and control of container securing arrangements for compliance with applicable strength requirements.

items.name: Excessive roll predicting
items.definition: Reducing risk of container loss by use of software predicting excessive roll motion.
items.commonDefinition: A predictive software application reducing the risk of container losses by predicting excessive roll motion.

items.name: Volume estimating
items.definition: Estimating the vessel's volume, based on measurements and calculations.
items.commonDefinition: An estimate of the vessel's volume, based on measurements and calculations.

items.name: Structural monitoring
items.definition: Monitoring the vessel's structure.
items.commonDefinition: Systems monitoring the vessel's structure.

items.name: Hull monitoring
items.definition:
Monitoring the structure globally and/or locally, by measuring
- loads, e.g. environment, propulsion
- responses, e.g. accelerations, strains, movements.
items.commonDefinition:
A system monitoring the structure globally and/or locally, by measuring
- loads, e.g. environment, propulsion
- responses, e.g. accelerations, strains, movements.

items.name: Loading conditions control
items.definition: Calculation and control of loading conditions for compliance with the applicable stability requirements and longitudinal and local strength requirements.
items.commonDefinition:
A computer based system for calculation and control of loading conditions for compliance with the applicable stability requirements and longitudinal and local strength requirements. The loading computer system consists of software (calculation program) and the computer (hardware) on which it runs.
Also denoted 'stability instrument'.

items.name: Draught and inclination monitoring
items.definition: Monitoring and indicating the draught and/or the inclination of the structure.
items.commonDefinition: A system monitoring and indicating the draught and/or the inclination of the structure.

items.name: Water ingress monitoring
items.definition: Monitoring the water levels in selected compartments, providing alarm if the water level exceeds predefined limits.
items.commonDefinition: A system monitoring the water levels in selected compartments, providing alarm if the water level exceeds predefined limits.

items.name: Anti-fouling
items.definition: Prevention of fouling, i.e. the accumulation of unwanted material on the hull. The fouling material can consist of either living organisms (biofouling) or a non-living substance (inorganic or organic).
items.commonDefinition: Arrangements, e.g. coating, for prevention of fouling, i.e. the accumulation of unwanted material on the hull. The fouling material can consist of either living organisms (biofouling) or a non-living substance (inorganic or organic).

items.name: Cargo handling, other
items.definition: Other arrangements related to cargo handling than those defined in functions 1001 - 1089.

items.name: Oil recovery
items.definition: Recovery of oil from a spillage at sea.
items.commonDefinition: A system for recovery of oil from a spillage at sea.

items.name: Oil recovery - equipment
items.definition: Equipment used for recovery of oil from a spillage at sea.
items.commonDefinition: Equipment used for recovery of oil from a spillage at sea.

items.name: Oil recovery equipment supporting
items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the oil recovery equipment.
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the oil recovery equipment.

items.name: Structural strength - ship
items.definition: Structural strength of a ship.
items.commonDefinition: The structure of a ship, e.g. hull, superstructure, deck houses, cross structures.

items.name: Structural strength - ship hull
items.definition: Structural strength of the ship hull, i.e. excluding superstructure, deck houses and other special purpose structures.
items.commonDefinition: The watertight body of the ship, i.e. excluding superstructure, deck houses and other special purpose structures.

items.name: Structural strength - ship decks
items.definition:
Structural strength and external/internal watertight integrity by fixed decks within the ship hull, excluding:
- decks within the superstructure and deck houses
- moveable decks
- helicopter decks
- inner bottom deck for vessels with double bottom.
A deck may cover the whole or a part of the ship's length or breadth. Decks are identified by numbers, starting at 1 for the lowermost deck.
items.commonDefinition:
All fixed decks within the ship hull, excluding:
- decks within the superstructure and deck houses
- moveable decks
- helicopter decks
- inner bottom deck for vessels with double bottom.
A deck may cover the whole or a part of the ship's length or breadth. Decks are identified by numbers, starting at 1 for the lowermost deck.

items.name: Structural strength - ship decks
items.definition:
Structural strength and external/internal watertight integrity by fixed decks within the ship hull, excluding:
- decks within the superstructure and deck houses
- moveable decks
- helicopter decks
- inner bottom deck for vessels with double bottom.
A deck may cover the whole or a part of the ship's length or breadth. Decks are identified by numbers, starting at 1 for the lowermost deck.
items.commonDefinition:
All fixed decks within the ship hull, excluding:
- decks within the superstructure and deck houses
- moveable decks
- helicopter decks
- inner bottom deck for vessels with double bottom.
A deck may cover the whole or a part of the ship's length or breadth. Decks are identified by numbers, starting at 1 for the lowermost deck.

items.name: Structural strength - ship transverse bulkheads
items.definition:
Structural strength and internal watertight integrity by transverse bulkheads within the ship hull, excluding:
- bulkheads within the superstructure and deck houses
- transom
- sternframe.
A transverse bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or breadth. Transverse bulkheads are identified by numbers, starting at 1 for the foremost (collision) bulkhead.
items.commonDefinition:
All transverse bulkheads within the ship hull, excluding:
- bulkheads within the superstructure and deck houses
- transom
- sternframe.
A transverse bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or breadth. Transverse bulkheads are identified by numbers, starting at 1 for the foremost (collision) bulkhead.

items.name: Structural strength - ship transverse bulkheads
items.definition:
Structural strength and internal watertight integrity by transverse bulkheads within the ship hull, excluding:
- bulkheads within the superstructure and deck houses
- transom
- sternframe.
A transverse bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or breadth. Transverse bulkheads are identified by numbers, starting at 1 for the foremost (collision) bulkhead.
items.commonDefinition:
All transverse bulkheads within the ship hull, excluding:
- bulkheads within the superstructure and deck houses
- transom
- sternframe.
A transverse bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or breadth. Transverse bulkheads are identified by numbers, starting at 1 for the foremost (collision) bulkhead.

items.name: Structural strength - ship transverse bulkheads, corrugated
items.definition:
Structural strength and internal watertight integrity by transverse corrugated bulkheads within the ship's cargo area  separating cargo compartments or ballast tanks.

A transverse bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or breadth. Transverse bulkheads are identified by numbers, starting at 1 for the foremost (collision) bulkhead.
items.commonDefinition:
All corrugated transverse bulkheads within the ship's cargo area  separating cargo compartments and/or ballast tanks.

A transverse bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or breadth. Transverse bulkheads are identified by numbers, starting at 1 for the foremost (collision) bulkhead.

items.name: Structural strength - ship longitudinal bulkheads
items.definition:
Structural strength and internal watertight integrity by longitudinal bulkheads within the ship hull, excluding:
- bulkheads within the superstructure and deck houses
- inner sides.
A longitudinal bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or length.
items.commonDefinition:
All longitudinal bulkheads within the ship hull, excluding:
- bulkheads within the superstructure and deck houses
- inner sides.
A longitudinal bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or length.

items.name: Structural strength - ship longitudinal bulkheads
items.definition:
Structural strength and internal watertight integrity by longitudinal bulkheads within the ship hull, excluding:
- bulkheads within the superstructure and deck houses
- inner sides.
A longitudinal bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or length.
items.commonDefinition:
All longitudinal bulkheads within the ship hull, excluding:

- inner sides.
A longitudinal bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or length.

items.name: Structural strength - ship longitudinal bulkheads corrugated
items.definition: Structural strength and internal watertight integrity by corrugated longitudinal bulkheads separating compartments within the cargo area. A longitudinal bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or length.
items.commonDefinition:
All corrugated longitudinal bulkheads separating compartments within the cargo area.
A longitudinal bulkhead may cover the whole or a part of the ship hull's height or length.

items.name: Structural strength - ship sides
items.definition: Structural strength and external/internal watertight integrity by sides and inner sides.
items.commonDefinition: All sides within the ship hull, as well inner as outer sides.

items.name: Structural strength - ship side shells
items.definition: Structural strength and external watertight integrity by watertight side boundaries between the vessel and the sea.
items.commonDefinition: The watertight side boundaries between the vessel and the sea.

items.commonDefinition: The structural and watertight element separating the ship from the sea on starboard side.

items.definition: Structural strength and watertight integrity by a longitudinal bulkhead separating the double side space from the cargo space/hold/tank.
items.commonDefinition: The longitudinal bulkhead separating the double side space from the cargo space/hold/tank.

items.name: Structural strength - ship inner side port

items.commonDefinition: Port inner side of a ship hull with double sides.

items.name: Structural strength - ship inner side starboard

items.commonDefinition: Starboard inner side of a ship hull with double sides.

items.definition: Structural strength and internal watertight integrity by vertical plate connecting the top wing sloping plate to the deck plate, starboard.

items.commonDefinition: Lower part of the ship's hull consisting the bottom, inner bottom (incl. Hopper), i.e. structure separating the cargo compartments from the sea below the ship.

items.name: Structural strength - ship bottom
items.definition: Structural strength and external watertight by bottom boundary between the vessel and the sea.
items.commonDefinition: A watertight bottom boundary between the vessel and the sea.

items.name: Structural strength - ship inner bottom
items.definition: Structural strength and internal watertight integrity by horizontal or sloping boundaries separating the double bottom compartment from the interior of the ship like cargo compartments, engine rooms etc.
items.commonDefinition: The horizontal or sloping boundaries separating the double bottom compartment from the interior of the ship like cargo compartments, engine rooms etc.

items.name: Structural strength - ship bottom inner

items.commonDefinition: Horizontal girder connecting sloping hopper plate to port side/inner side.

items.name: Structural strength - hopper horizontal girder starboard
items.definition: Structural strength and internal watertight integrity by a horizontal girder connecting the sloping hopper plate to starboard side/inner side.
items.commonDefinition: Horizontal girder connecting sloping hopper plate to starboard side/inner side.

items.name: Structural strength - ship structures other
items.definition: Structural strength of structures within the ship's hull other than decks, bulkheads, sides, bow, stern, bottoms and moonpools.
items.commonDefinition: Structures within the ship's hull other than decks, bulkheads, sides, bow, stern, bottoms and moonpools.

items.name: Structural strength - ship internal structures other than decks and bulkheads

items.name: Structural strength - ship superstructures
items.definition:
Structural strength of all decked structures on the freeboard deck, extending from side to side of the vessel or with their side plating not being inboard of the shell plating more than 4% of the breadth.
A raised quarter deck is regarded as a superstructure.
items.commonDefinition:
All decked structures on the freeboard deck, extending from side to side of the vessel or with their side plating not being inboard of the shell plating more than 4% of the breadth.
A raised quarter deck is regarded as a superstructure.

items.name: Hydrocarbon production incoming flow
items.definition: Flowing of hydrocarbons from the production wells to the vessel.

items.name: Hydrocarbon production well shut down
items.definition: Control of hydrocarbon flow from or injection to subsea reservoirs.
items.commonDefinition:
Series of gate valve assemblies with additional choking used to control the flow of hydrocarbons from or injection to subsea reservoirs. The christmas trees shall contain all loads from temperature, pressure, fluid etc. The equipment may be located subsea or at the surface on a facility.
See also NORSOK D-010.

items.name: Hydrocarbon production christmas tree
items.definition: Control of hydrocarbon flow from or injection to a subsea reservoir.
items.commonDefinition:
A series of gate valve assemblies with additional choking used to control the flow of hydrocarbons from or injection to a subsea reservoir. The christmas tree shall contain all loads from temperature, pressure, fluid etc. The equipment may be located subsea or at the surface on a facility.
See also NORSOK D-010.

items.name: Hydrocarbon transfer from seabed to vessel
items.definition: Incoming flow of hydrocarbons from seabed to vessel.

items.name: Hydrocarbon production riser quick disconnection
items.commonName: Hydrocarbon production riser quick disconnection system

items.name: Hydrocarbon production riser swivel
items.definition: Transfer of hydrocarbon and other media flow through a swivel located within the turret that accommodates vessel rotation with respect to the seabed.

items.name: Hydrocarbon production flow collection - manifolds
items.definition: Connection of incoming hydrocarbon production flow, and distribution of the flow to the separation process.

items.name: Structural strength - column stabilised unit
items.definition: Structural strength of a column stabilised unit.
items.commonDefinition:
The structure of a column stabilised unit, e.g. pontoons, columns, braces, upper hull, deck houses and their connections.
A column stabilised unit obtains most of its buoyancy from ballasted, watertight pontoons located below the ocean surface and wave action. Structural columns connect the pontoons and upper hull. The upper hull can be located high above the sea level.
With its hull structure submerged at a deep draught, a column stabilised unit is less affected by wave loadings than a ship. With a small water-plane area, however, the semi-submersible is sensitive to load changes, and therefore must be carefully trimmed to maintain stability. Unlike a submersible, a semi-submersible vessel is not supported by resting on the seabed.
Column stabilised units are able to transform from a deep to a shallow draught by deballasting (removing ballast water from the hull), thereby becoming surface vessels. Usually they are moved from location to location in this configuration.

items.name: Blowout prevention
items.definition:
Primary functions:
- confine well fluid to the wellbore
- provide means to add fluid to the wellbore
- allow controlled volumes of fluid to be withdrawn from the wellbore.
Additional functions:
 - regulate and monitor wellbore pressure
- center and hang off the drill string in the wellbore
- shut in the well (e.g. seal the void, annulus, between drillpipe and casing)
- kill the well (prevent the flow of formation fluid, influx, from the reservoir into the wellbore)
- seal the wellhead (close off the wellbore)
- sever the casing or drill pipe (in case of emergencies).

items.name: Well isolation
items.definition: Preventing uncontrolled escape of hydrocarbons either in the annular space between the casing and the drill pipe or in open hole (for example, hole with no drill pipe) during drilling or completion operations.
items.commonDefinition: One or more valves installed at the wellhead to prevent the uncontrolled escape of hydrocarbons either in the annular space between the casing and the drill pipe or in open hole (for example, hole with no drill pipe) during drilling or completion operations.

items.name: Disconnection of marine riser from blowout preventer stack
items.definition: Disconnection of the marine riser from the blowout preventer stack in an emergency situation, and sealing of the marine riser in order to avoid discharge of drilling fluid.
items.commonDefinition: An arrangement of connector, annulars, flexible joints and valves for disconnection of the marine riser from the blowout preventer stack in an emergency situation, and sealing of the marine riser in order to avoid discharge of drilling fluid.

items.name: Drilling fluid mixing and storage
items.definition: Mixing of drilling fluid based on e.g. baryte, bentonite and water. The main functions of drilling fluids include providing hydrostatic pressure to prevent formation fluids from entering into the well bore, keeping the drill bit cool and clean during drilling, carrying out drill cuttings, and suspending the drill cuttings while drilling is paused and when the drilling assembly is brought in and out of the hole. The drilling fluid used for a particular job is selected to avoid formation damage and to limit corrosion.
items.commonDefinition: A system for mixing of drilling fluid based on e.g. baryte, bentonite and water. The main functions of drilling fluids include providing hydrostatic pressure to prevent formation fluids from entering into the well bore, keeping the drill bit cool and clean during drilling, carrying out drill cuttings, and suspending the drill cuttings while drilling is paused and when the drilling assembly is brought in and out of the hole. The drilling fluid used for a particular job is selected to avoid formation damage and to limit corrosion.

items.name: Well testing arrangements
items.commonDefinition:
Arrangements needed to test the well, i.e. to execute a set of planned data acquisition activities to broaden the knowledge and understanding of hydrocarbons properties and characteristics of the underground reservoir where hydrocarbons are trapped. The test will also provide information about the state of the particular well used to collect data. The overall objective is identifying the reservoir's capacity to produce hydrocarbons, such as oil, natural gas and condensate.
Data gathered during the test period includes volumetric flow rate and pressure observed in the selected well. Outcomes of a well test, for instance flow rate data and gas oil ratio data, may support the well allocation process for an ongoing production phase, while other data about the reservoir capabilities will support reservoir management.

items.name: Structural strength - self elevating unit
items.definition: Structural strength of a self elevating unit.
items.commonDefinition:
The structure of a self elevating unit, consisting of legs with their foundations, the hull, jack houses, cantilevers and deck houses.
A self elevating unit is a mobile platform consisting of a buoyant hull fitted with a number of movable legs, capable of raising its hull over the surface of the sea. The buoyant hull enables transportation of the unit and all attached machinery to a desired location. Once on location the hull is raised to the required elevation above the sea surface supported by the sea bed. The legs of such units may be designed to penetrate the sea bed, may be fitted with enlarged sections or footings, or may be attached to a bottom mat

items.definition: Structural strength of helicopter deck arrangement.
items.commonDefinition:
A structural arrangement providing for helicopters:
- an erected landing platform, or
- a landing area directly on the weather deck or on the top of a superstructure or deck house.
The arrangement may consist of:
- the landing area, i.e. the helicopter deck
- substructures transferring loads from the helicopter deck to the main structure
- strengthening of the main structure ( supporting structure).

items.name: Helicopter deck - structural strength
items.definition: Structural strength of helicopter deck.
items.commonDefinition: A landing area for helicopters.

items.name: Helicopter deck galvanic corrosion prevention
items.definition: Connecting structural elements of the helicopter deck arrangement made of different materials, while preventing galvanic corrosion.
items.commonDefinition: Connection devices for use between structural elements of the helicopter deck arrangement made of different materials, in order to prevent galvanic corrosion.

items.name: Helicopter deck substructure - structural strength
items.definition: Transferring loads from the helicopter deck to the main structure.
items.commonDefinition: Structural elements transferring loads from the helicopter deck to the main structure.

items.name: Helicopter deck supporting structure - structural strength
items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the helicopter deck.
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the helicopter deck.

items.name: Hangar arrangement - structural strength
items.definition: Structural strength of hangar arrangement.
items.commonDefinition:
A structural arrangement providing for aircraft:
- an enclosed structure for holding aircraft
- strengthening of the main structure ( supporting structure).

items.name: Hangar - structural strength
items.definition: Structural strength of hangar.
items.commonDefinition: An enclosed structure for holding aircraft.

items.name: Hangar supporting structure - structural strength
items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the hangar.
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the hangar.

items.name: Topside load transfer
items.definition: Transfer of loads and moments from the topside structure to the vessel structure.

items.name: Topside module structure supporting
items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the topside structure.
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the topside structure.

items.name: Access through shell by doors and ramps
items.definition: Access through the shell by doors or ramps in the vessel's bow, sides or stern, together with associated functions as doors supporting, power supply and control and monitoring.
items.commonDefinition: Doors or ramps in the vessel's bow, sides or stern, together with supporting structures for the doors, power supply and control and monitoring systems.

items.name: Access through shell by bow doors
items.definition: Access through the shell by doors or ramps in the vessel's bow. The bow door arrangements will always include an outer door, and may include an inner door or ramp.
items.commonDefinition: Doors or ramps in the vessel's bow and their supporting structures. The bow door arrangements will always include an outer door, and may include an inner door or ramp.

items.name: Access through shell by outer bow doors
items.definition: Access through the shell by the outer door in the vessel's bow
items.commonDefinition: Outer door in the vessel's bow

items.commonDefinition: Inner door or ramp in the vessel's bow

items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for the doors and ramps in the vessel's bow.

items.name: Access through shell by side door arrangements
items.definition: Access through the shell by doors or ramps in the vessel's sides, and supporting of the doors.
items.commonDefinition: Doors or ramps in the vessel's sides and their supporting structures.

items.commonDefinition: A doors or ramp in the vessels side.

items.commonDefinition: A door or ramp in the vessel's sides, and its supporting structure.

items.name: Access through shell by stern door arrangements
items.definition: Access through the shell by a door or ramp in the vessel's stern, and supporting of the door.
items.commonDefinition: Doors or ramps in the vessel's stern and their supporting structures.

items.name: Access through shell by stern doors
items.definition: Access through the shell by a door or ramp in the vessel's stern.
items.commonDefinition: A door or ramp in the vessel's stern.

items.name: Stern doors supporting
items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the stern door or ramp.
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structure for the door or ramp in the vessel's stern.

items.name: Shell doors power supplying
items.definition: Supplying power to operate the shell doors.
items.commonDefinition: A system supplying power to operate the shell doors.

items.name: Shell doors control and monitoring
items.commonName: Shell doors control and monitoring system
items.definition:
- controlling opening and closing
- monitoring open / closed status
 for doors and ramps in the vessel's external boundaries.
items.commonDefinition:
A system
- controlling opening and closing
- monitoring open / closed status
 for doors and ramps in the vessel's external boundaries.

items.name: Shell doors water leakage detection
items.commonName: Shell doors water leakage detection alarm system
items.definition:
- detecting water leakage through shell doors
- providing alarm when detecting
items.commonDefinition:
A system
- detecting water leakage through shell doors
- providing alarm upon detection.

items.name: Shell doors television monitoring
items.definition: Television surveillance in order to provide an indication to navigation bridge and engine control room of leakage and monitor the position of the doors and their closing arrangements.
items.commonDefinition: Television surveillance in order to provide an indication to navigation bridge and engine control room of leakage and monitor the position of the doors and their closing arrangements.

items.name: Access through weathertight doors
items.definition: Access through weathertight doors in superstructure end bulkheads, freeboard decks or superstructure decks.
items.commonDefinition: Weathertight doors for access through superstructure end bulkheads, freeboard decks or superstructure decks.

items.name: Light admission
items.definition:
Admission of natural light to the vessel's interior, and viewing of the outside world.
When arranged by use of windows that may be opened, admission of air and access may also be possible.
items.commonDefinition: Transparent parts of the vessel, used to admit light and to provide a view to the outside world. When open used to admit air, when closed used to provide a strong water-tight, weather-tight, and sometimes light-tight barrier.

items.name: Light admission through windows
items.definition:
Admission of natural light through windows, and viewing of the outside world.
When arranged by use of windows that may be opened, admission of air and access may also be possible.
items.commonDefinition: Transparent part of the vessel, used to admit light and to provide a view to the outside world. When open used to admit air, when closed used to provide a strong water-tight, weather-tight, and sometimes light-tight barrier.

items.name: Light admission through side scuttles
items.definition:
Admission of natural light through small, generally circular, windows in the hull, and viewing of the outside world.
When arranged by use of windows that may be opened, admission of air and access may also be possible.
items.commonDefinition: Small, generally circular, windows in the hull used to admit light and to provide a view to the outside world. When open used to admit air, when closed used to provide a strong water-tight, weather-tight, and sometimes light-tight barrier.

items.name: Access to cargo holds
items.definition:
Access to cargo holds, by means of:
- cargo hatches
- cargo hatches power supply
- cargo hatches control and monitoring system.
items.commonDefinition:
Arrangements for access to cargo holds, consisting of:
- cargo hatches
- cargo hatches power supply
- cargo hatches control and monitoring system.

items.definition: Access through the shell by means of cargo hatches, i.e. hatches for cargo loading and unloading.
items.commonDefinition: Hatches in the shell for cargo loading and unloading.

items.definition: Access through the shell by means of a cargo hatch, i.e. a hatch for cargo loading and unloading.
items.commonDefinition: A hatch in the shell for cargo loading and unloading.

items.name: Cargo hatches power supplying
items.definition: Supplying power to operate the cargo hatches.

items.definition:
- controlling opening and closing
- monitoring open / closed status
 for cargo hatches.
items.commonDefinition:
A system
- controlling opening and closing
- monitoring open / closed status
 for cargo hatches.

items.name: Access through service hatchways
items.definition: Arrangements for access through the superstructure end bulkheads, freeboard decks or superstructure decks by means of service hatches, i.e. hatches for other purposes than cargo loading and unloading. Each arrangement consists of a hatch and corresponding coamings.
items.commonDefinition: Hatch arrangements in superstructure end bulkheads, freeboard decks or superstructure decks for other purposes than cargo loading and unloading. Each arrangement consists of a hatch and corresponding coamings.

items.name: Access through service hatches

items.commonDefinition: Hatches in the superstructure end bulkheads, freeboard decks or superstructure decks for other purposes than cargo loading and unloading.

items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the service hatches
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the service hatches.

Watertight doors or ramps in internal watertight decks or bulkheads, including

items.name: Buoyancy control
items.definition: Controlling the buoyancy of the vessel.
items.commonDefinition: Devices controlling the buoyancy of the vessel.

items.definition: Increasing the buoyancy of the vessel by attaching a rigid material with a density lower than the surrounding medium.
items.commonDefinition: A rigid material  attached to the vessel, with a density lower than the surrounding medium, thereby increasing the buoyancy of the vessel.

items.name: Buoyancy decreasing by a weight
items.definition: Decreasing the buoyancy of the vessel by attaching a rigid material with a density higher than the surrounding medium.
items.commonDefinition: A rigid material with a density higher than the surrounding medium attached to the vessel, thereby decreasing the buoyancy of the vessel.

items.name: Buoyancy increasing by jettisoning
items.definition: Increasing the buoyancy of the vessel by removing heavy objects.
items.commonDefinition: A system for removing heavy objects from the vessel, thereby increasing the buoyancy.

items.commonDefinition: Tanks for controlling the vessel's buoyancy through control of the amount of water contained.

items.commonDefinition: An integral tank for controlling the vessel's buoyancy through control of the amount of water contained.

items.commonDefinition: Pressure vessels for controlling the vessel's buoyancy through control of the amount of water contained.

items.definition: Connections from deck to double bottom tanks.

items.name: Access internal by a trunk
items.definition: Connection from deck to double bottom tank.

items.name: Access, external
items.commonName: External access to and from vessel
items.definition: Access to the vessel from outside, e.g. from a pilot boat or quay.

items.name: Access external by accommodation ladder - arrangement
items.definition: Access between the vessel and the quay, or between the vessel and another vessel.
items.commonDefinition:
An arrangement enabling access between the vessel and the quay, or between the vessel and another vessel. The arrangement will normally consist of:
- the accommodation ladder, including upper platform, decking, steps, guard rails, and lower platform
- hoisting and lowering arrangement, i.e. winch, davit and falls.

items.name: Access external by accommodation ladder - ladder
items.definition: Use of a ladder (rigid stair) to enable access between the vessel and the quay, or between the vessel and another vessel. The accommodation ladder is normally mounted parallel to the vessel's side.

items.name: Access external by accommodation ladder - davit
items.definition: Support of the accommodation ladder falls.

items.name: Accommodation ladder line hoisting, lowering and holding
items.definition: Hoisting, lowering, holding and/or storing the accommodation ladder line.
items.commonDefinition: A mechanical device for hoisting, lowering, holding and/or storing the accommodation ladder line.

items.name: Transferring tensile forces between vessel and accommodation ladder
items.definition: Transferring tensile forces between vessel and accommodation ladder, to hoist, lower and hold the accommodation ladder.
items.commonDefinition: Lines (chains, wires or ropes), transferring tensile forces between vessel and accommodation ladder, to hoist, lower and hold the accommodation ladder.

normalAssignmentNames.C982: Accommodation ladder hoisting steel wire rope segments
normalAssignmentNames.C983: Accommodation ladder hoisting fibre rope segments

items.name: Access external by accommodation ladder - supporting structure
items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate the loads from the accommodation ladder.
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate the loads from the accommodation ladder.

items.name: Access external by gangway
items.definition: The use of a gangway to enable access between a vessel and the quay, or between the vessel and another vessel/installation.
items.commonDefinition:
A bridge structure to allow safe embarkation and disembarkation from vessel to shore or access to another vessel/installation.
The gangway is normally carried on board as a loose item, and hoisted into location when used. The top is locked to the vessel, while the bottom is resting on the quay or the other vessel/installation upon a roller.  The gangway is normally mounted orthogonally to the vessel's side. The gangway is equipped with decking, threads and guard rails. See also ISO 7061:1993(E).

items.name: Gangway loads and moments transfer to vessel
items.definition: Transferring loads from the gangway to the vessel's main structure.
items.commonDefinition: Devices transferring loads from the gangway to the vessel's main structure.

items.name: Gangway supporting
items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate the loads from the gang way.
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate the loads from the gangway.

items.name: Access external by personnel transfer arrangement
items.definition:
An arrangement enabling access between the vessel and the marine installation. The arrangement will normally consist of:
- the transfer arrangement, including platform, decking, steps, guard rails
- hoisting and lowering arrangement, hydraulic or mechanical.
items.commonDefinition:
An arrangement enabling access between the vessel and the marine installation. The arrangement will normally consist of:
- the transfer arrangement, including platform, decking, steps, guard rails

items.name: Mooring
items.definition:
Fastening the vessel with respect to external rigid structures as quays or buoys, by the use of mechanical connections between the vessel and the external rigid structures.
Note 1: This function includes 'Single point mooring'.
Note 2: Holding the vessel with respect to the seabed is covered by function 323 ‘Position mooring’.
items.commonDefinition:
Arrangements for fastening the vessel with respect to external rigid structures as quays or buoys, by the use of mechanical connections between the vessel and the external rigid structures.
Note 1: This function includes 'Single point mooring'.
Note 2: Holding the vessel with respect to the seabed is covered by function 323 ‘Position mooring’.
