items.commonDefinition: Fairleads located in the vessel's bow, with the purpose of controlling the mooring line's direction.

items.name: Mooring by tendon arrangements
items.definition: Permanent mooring of an offshore installation by means of tendons connecting the installation and a set of tendon foundations fixed to the sea bottom. The tendons must have relatively high axial stiffness (low elasticity), such that virtually all vertical motion of the installation is eliminated.
items.commonDefinition: Arrangements for permanent mooring of an offshore installation by means of tendons connecting the installation and a set of tendon foundations fixed to the sea bottom. The tendons must have relatively high axial stiffness (low elasticity), such that virtually all vertical motion of the installation is eliminated.

items.name: Load transfer from tendon to sea bottom

items.commonDefinition: A structure transferring loads from the tendon to the sea bottom.

items.name: Load transfer from vessel to foundation by a group of tendons

items.commonDefinition: Cables with high axial stiffness connecting an offshore installation with a foundation on the sea bottom.

items.definition: Connecting an offshore installation with a foundation on the sea bottom.

items.definition: Permanent mooring of an offshore installation by means of tendons connecting the installation and a set of tendon foundations fixed to the sea bottom. items.name: Masts and masts equipment securing
items.definition:
Carriage of rigging:
- standing rigging, i.e. lines, wires, or rods which are more or less fixed in position. Standing rigging is placed under tension to keep the masts securely in position and adequately braced to handle loads induced by e.g. wind, vessel movement or vibrations.
- running rigging, which represents elements of rigging which move and change fairly often during operation.
- running rigging, which represents elements of rigging which move and change fairly often during operation.
items.commonDefinition:
- standing rigging, i.e. lines, wires, or rods which are more or less fixed in position. standing rigging is placed under tension to keep the masts securely in position and adequately braced to handle loads induced by e.g. wind, vessel movement or vibrations.
- running rigging, which represents elements of rigging which move and change fairly often during operation.

items.name: Propulsion and steering general
items.definition:
General items relating to:
Propulsion: The ability to move a vessel though the water, forwards or backwards, and to change the vessel's speed.
Steering: The ability to change the direction of the vessel's movement, i.e. to turn the vessel.

items.name: Propulsion and steering
items.definition:
Propulsion: The ability to move a vessel though the water, forwards or backwards, and to change the vessel's speed.
Steering: The ability to change the direction of the vessel's movement, i.e. to rotate the vessel around a vertical axis.
items.commonDefinition:
Propulsion arrangements: Arrangements enabling the vessel to move though the water, forwards or backwards, and to change the vessel's speed.
Steering arrangements: Arrangements enabling the vessel to change the direction of movement, i.e. to rotate the vessel around a vertical axis.

items.name: Machinery control devices locating
items.definition: The location from where the propulsion and steering equipment is controlled.

items.name: Propulsion control and monitoring
items.definition:
Control and monitoring of the propulsion, as well conventional propulsion (one or more propulsion lines with a propeller in the vessel's stern) as thruster propulsion, from as well remote as local positions.
Excluding control and monitoring systems that are parts of the propulsion products, e.g. engines or thrusters, but including the interface to such systems.
items.commonDefinition:
Control and monitoring system for the propulsion, as well conventional propulsion (one or more propulsion lines with a propeller in the vessel's stern) as thruster propulsion, from as well remote as local positions.
Excluding control and monitoring systems that are parts of the propulsion products, e.g. engines or thrusters, but including the interface to such systems.

items.name: Steering control and monitoring
items.definition:
Control and monitoring of the steering, as well conventional steering (by means of rudders) as thruster steering, from as well remote as local positions.
Excluding control and monitoring systems that are parts of the steering products, e.g. rudder actuators or thrusters, but including the interface to such systems.
items.commonDefinition:
Control and monitoring system for the steering, as well conventional steering (by means of rudders) as thruster steering, from as well remote as local positions.
Excluding control and monitoring systems that are parts of the steering products, e.g. rudder actuators or thrusters, but including the interface to such systems.

items.name: Heading controlling
items.definition: Enabling a vessel to keep a preset heading with minimum operation of the steering gear.
items.commonDefinition:
A system enabling a vessel to keep a preset heading with minimum operation of the steering gear.
Also known as 'autopilot'.

items.name: Track control
items.definition: Keeping the vessel on a pre-determined track throughout its passage.
items.commonDefinition:
A system designed to keep the vessel on a pre-determined track throughout its passage.
The system has to be interfaced with a position fixing system.
A track control systems will include the functional capabilities of a heading control system.

items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the intermediate shaft bearings.

items.name: Propulsion, conventional - shaft coupling
items.definition: Connecting shaft ends, or shafts and another member together and transmit relevant forces from one shaft to another member of the propulsion line. A coupling may be stiff, or may be elastic in axial and/or radial, and/or torsional direction. This function shall not be used for propeller connections.

normalAssignmentNames.C557: Propulsion shaft connection, cylindrical with conical sleeve

- preventing sea water ingress through the propeller shaft hull penetration
- supporting the propeller shaft.

- transferring torque and thrust from the propulsion line intermediate shaft to the propeller
- preventing sea water ingress through the propeller shaft hull penetration
- supporting the propeller shaft.

items.name: Propeller shaft rotation enabling at stern tube forward side
items.definition:
At the forward side of the stern tube:
- enabling rotation between the propeller shaft and the vessel while constraining other relative motion types
- reducing friction between the propeller shaft and the vessel, and
- transferring radial forces between the propeller shaft and the vessel.

A mechanical device located at the forward side of the stern tube,

- reducing friction between the propeller shaft and the vessel, and
- transferring radial forces between the propeller shaft and the vessel.

items.definition:
- preventing sea water contact with the propeller shaft, thereby preventing corrosion
- preventing wear of the propeller shaft by letting the liner take the wear.
items.commonDefinition:
A hollow cylinder mounted on the surface of the propeller shaft within the stern tube arrangement fully or partly
- preventing sea water contact with the propeller shaft, thereby preventing corrosion
- preventing wear of the propeller shaft by letting the liner take the wear.

items.name: Steering arrangements, rudder based
items.definition:
Enabling the vessel to change the direction of movement, i.e. to rotate the vessel around a vertical axis, by means of rudder arrangements.
Steering by thrusters is not included.
items.commonDefinition:
Rudder based arrangements enabling the vessel to change the direction of movement, i.e. to rotate the vessel around a vertical axis.
Each steering arrangement consists of a rudder, its steering gear and their supporting structures. Steering by thrusters is not included.

items.name: Steering arrangement, rudder based
items.definition:
Enabling the vessel to change the direction of movement, i.e. to rotate the vessel around a vertical axis, by means of a rudder arrangement.
Steering by thrusters is not included.
items.commonDefinition:
A rudder based arrangement enabling the vessel to change the direction of movement, i.e. to rotate the vessel around a vertical axis.
The steering arrangement consists of a rudder, its steering gear and their supporting structures. Steering by thrusters is not included.

items.name: Arrangement for steering by means of a rudder.
items.definition: Arrangement for steering the vessel by means of a rudder.
items.commonDefinition:
Arrangement for steering the vessel by means of a rudder, consisting of as applicable:
- rudder trunk
- rudder
- rudder horn
- sole piece
- rudder supporting structure.

items.name: Rudder stock locating
items.definition: Locating the upper part of the rudder stock.
items.commonDefinition: The space beneath the rudder carrier where the upper part of the rudder stock is located.

items.name: Steering by rudder
items.definition: Steering the vessel by redirecting the fluid past the hull, thus imparting a turning or yawing moment to the vessel.
items.commonDefinition: A device for steering the vessel by redirecting the fluid past the hull, thus imparting a turning or yawing moment to the vessel.

items.name: Horn pintle supporting
items.definition: Supporting the horn pintle of a semi-spade rudder.

items.name: Heel pintle supporting
items.definition: Supporting the heel pintle of a balanced rudder.
items.commonDefinition: A vessel appendage on which the heel pintle of a balanced rudder is mounted.

items.name: Rudder supporting
items.definition: Strengthening of the main vessel structure, e.g. a deck, in order to accommodate loads and moments from the rudder.
items.commonDefinition: Supporting structures for the rudders, except rudder horn and sole piece.

items.name: Arrangement providing torsional moments acting on the rudder

Arrangement transforming input power to torsional moments acting on the rudder, consisting of:

- steering gear

- steering gear supporting structure.

items.definition: A propulsion and steering thruster arrangement, including a thruster and possibly its separate driver and torque transmission equipment. The term thruster includes pods or waterjets.

items.name: Propulsion thruster driver arrangement
items.definition:
A driver (e.g. diesel engine, steam turbine, gas turbine or electric power unit) connected to a propulsion thruster, and its supporting structure.
In case of diesel electric propulsion, the diesel engine shall be defined as the main generator driver, while the propulsion thruster driver shall be an electric power unit.

items.name: Locating equipment for position keeping control
items.definition: Locating equipment designated for position keeping operations, where relevant indicators, displays, alarm panels, control panels and internal communication systems are installed.

items.name: Position keeping control and monitoring
items.commonName: Position keeping control and monitoring systems
items.definition:
Controlling the vessel's position and heading
- through control of the vessel's propulsion, steering and manoeuvring facilities
- based on user inputs for wanted position and heading
- using measurements of the vessel's position, heading and movements, external environmental parameters and where relevant anchor system parameters as inputs.
items.commonDefinition:
Systems controlling the vessel's position and heading

- based on user inputs for wanted position and heading
- using measurements of the vessel's position, heading and movements, external environmental parameters and where relevant anchor system parameters as inputs.

- using measurements of the vessel's position, heading and movements and external environmental parameters as inputs.

items.name: Thruster assisted mooring simulation
items.definition:
Provision through simulation of:
- mooring conditions on input of proposed anchor pattern and anchor line tensions
- effects of changing weather conditions
- anchor line tensions, low frequency motions, wave frequency motions and final position caused by anchor line failure
- relevant functions both with and without thruster assistance.
items.commonDefinition:
A simulation facility providing:
- mooring conditions on input of proposed anchor pattern and anchor line tensions
- effects of changing weather conditions
- anchor line tensions, low frequency motions, wave frequency motions and final position caused by anchor line failure
- relevant functions both with and without thruster assistance.

items.name: Thruster control mode selection
items.definition: Selecting the thruster control mode, i.e. manual, independent joystick or automatic.
items.commonDefinition:
A system for selecting the thruster control mode, i.e. manual, independent joystick or automatic.
The system may consist of a single selector switch or individual selectors for each thruster (DP).

items.name: Motion measuring
items.definition:
Measuring the vessel's dynamic motions with respect to a coordinate system that travels with the vessel.
Translational motions:
- heave: linear vertical motion
- sway: linear horisontal transverse motion
- surge: linear horisontal longitudinal motion
Rotational motions:
- roll: rotation about the longitudinal horisontal axis. A static roll angle is referred to as list or heel.
- pitch: rotation about the transverse horisontal axis. A static pitch angle is referred to as trim.
- yaw: rotation about the vertical axis.
items.commonDefinition:
A system measuring the vessel's dynamic motions with respect to a coordinate system that travels with the vessel.
Translational motions:
- heave: linear vertical motion
- sway: linear horisontal transverse motion
- surge: linear horisontal longitudinal motion
Rotational motions:
- roll: rotation about the longitudinal horisontal axis. A static roll angle is referred to as list or heel.
- pitch: rotation about the transverse horisontal axis. A static pitch angle is referred to as trim.
- yaw: rotation about the vertical axis.

items.name: Earthing
items.definition:
Provision of connection to a common electric potential (earth), for:
- protection against electrical hazards
- a common return path for electric current
- a reference point in an electrical circuit from which other voltages are measured
On a vessel, earth will normally be the hull.
items.commonDefinition:
Connection arrangements to a common electric potential (earth), for:

- a common return path for electric current
- a reference point in an electrical circuit from which other voltages are measured
On a vessel, earth will normally be the hull.

items.name: Lightning protection
items.definition: Protection of the vessel's equipment and crew from damage due to lightning strikes by intercepting such strikes and safely passing their extremely high voltage currents to earth.

items.name: Fuel gas containment in independent tanks type A
items.commonName: Fuel gas independent tank arrangements type A
items.definition: fuel gas containment in independent tank arrangements of type A.
items.commonDefinition:
Arrangements for fuel gas containment in independent tanks of type A. Independent implies that the tanks are self-supporting, do not form part of the vessel's main structure and are not essential to the strength if the vessel's main structure.
Type A implies that the tanks are designed primarily using classical ship-structural analysis procedures.
Each arrangement may include:
- an independent tank, the tank boundaries are the primary barriers of the fuel gas containment system
- thermal insulation
- supports for the independent tank
- secondary barriers for the fuel gas containment system
- a hold space surrounding the independent tank
- supporting structures accommodating loads and moments from the independent tank.

items.commonName: Fuel gas independent tank type A supports
items.definition: Transfer of loads from an independent tank of type A to the vessel's main structure, i.e. the inner hull.
items.commonDefinition:
Supports for transfer of loads from an independent tank of type A to the vessel's main structure, i.e. the inner hull.
The tank should be supported in a manner which will prevent bodily movement of the tank under static and dynamic loads while allowing contraction and expansion of the tank under temperature variations and hull deflections without due stressing of the tank and of the hull.

Containing fuel not intended for the vessel’s propulsion and power generation, selection of tank types:
- integral, i.e. tanks that are an integral part of the vessel's structure
- independent, i.e. tanks that are independent from the vessel's structure.
items.commonDefinition:
Selection between tank types for containment of fuel not intended for the vessel’s propulsion and power generation:
- integral, i.e. tanks that are an integral part of the vessel's structure
- independent, i.e. tanks that are independent from the vessel's structure.

items.name: Low flashpoint liquid fuel containment and distribution
items.definition:
Containment, distribution and treatment of ow flashpoint liquid fuel for combustion purposes, and control and monitoring of these functions:
- containment: storing and service in tanks
- distribution: bunkering, transfer between tanks and distribution to consumers.
items.commonDefinition:
A system for containment and distribution of low flashpoint liquid fuel for combustion purposes, and control and monitoring of these functions
- containment: tanks for storing and service
- distribution: bunkering, transfer between tanks and distribution to consumers.

items.name: Low flashpoint liquid fuel system control and monitoring
items.commonName: Low flashpoint liquid fuel control and monitoring system
items.definition:
Control and monitoring of the low flashpoint liquid fuel system:
- control of equipment as pumps, compressors and valves
- monitoring of parameters as temperatures, pressure, flow
- automatic shutdown of the low flashpoint liquid fuel system based on detected parameter values exceeding predefined limits.
items.commonDefinition:
A system for control and monitoring of the low flashpoint liquid fuel system:
- control of equipment as pumps, compressors and valves
- monitoring of parameters as temperatures, pressure, flow
- automatic shutdown of the low flashpoint liquid fuel system based on detected parameter values exceeding predefined limits.

items.name: Sea and fresh water supply, containment and distribution
items.definition:
Supply, containment and distribution of sea and fresh water for any purpose, except:
- containment and distribution of ballast water, see function 652.
- distribution of fire water, see function 841.

items.name: Sea water supply, distribution and discharging
items.definition:
Supply, containment and distribution of sea water used for any purpose, e.g.:
- cooling of fresh water, machinery and cargo
- fresh water generation
- inert gas scrubbers and water seals
- exhaust gas scrubbers
- cargo heating
Exceptions:
- containment and distribution of ballast water, see function 652.
- distribution of fire water, see function 841.
items.commonDefinition:
Systems for supply, containment and distribution of sea water used for any purpose, e.g.:
- cooling of fresh water, machinery and cargo
- fresh water generation
- inert gas scrubbers and water seals
- exhaust gas scrubbers
- cargo heating
Exceptions:
- containment and distribution of ballast water, see function 652.
- distribution of fire water, see function 841.

items.name: Sewage containing, treating and discharging
items.definition:
Containment, treatment and discharging of sewage, i.e.:
- drainage and other wastes from any form of toilets and urinals
- drainage from medical premises (dispensary, sick bay etc.) via wash basins, wash tubs and scuppers located in such premises
- drainage from spaces containing live animals
- other waste waters when mixed with the drainages defined above.
See MARPOL Annex IV.
items.commonDefinition:
A system for containment, treatment and discharging of sewage, i.e.:
- drainage and other wastes from any form of toilets and urinals
- drainage from medical premises (dispensary, sick bay etc.) via wash basins, wash tubs and scuppers located in such premises
- drainage from spaces containing live animals
- other waste waters when mixed with the drainages defined above
See MARPOL Annex IV.
The system will normally consist of:
- piping
- pumping units
- tanks
- sewage treatment units.

items.name: Machinery and marine tanks pressure, level and overflow control
items.definition:
For machinery and marine tanks, including void spaces:
- prevention of over- and under-pressure
- determination of liquid level
- control of spillage in case of overfilling.
items.commonDefinition:
For machinery and marine tanks, including void spaces:
- Air pipes with vent heads for the prevention of over- and under-pressure with respect to the atmosphere within machinery and marine tanks, while ensuring watertight integrity
- Sounding systems for the determination of liquid levels
- Arrangements for control of spillage in case of overfilling machinery and marine tanks.

items.name: Machinery and marine tanks over- and under-pressure prevention
items.definition:
Over- and under-pressure prevention arrangements for machinery and marine tanks including void spaces, while upholding watertight integrity.
Over- and under-pressure prevention of cargo tanks is covered by function 1025.
items.commonDefinition:
Air pipes with vent heads for the prevention of over- and under-pressure with respect to the atmosphere within machinery and marine tanks, while ensuring watertight integrity.
Over- and under-pressure prevention is important during filling and discharging of the tanks, and loss of this function may cause structural failure or deformation of the tanks.
Over- and under-pressure prevention of cargo tanks is covered by function 1025.

items.name: Machinery and marine tanks over- and under-pressure prevention - atmospheric connection
items.definition: Conducting air for over- and under-pressure prevention for machinery and marine tanks.
items.commonDefinition: Piping (i.e. pipes, pipe connections, valves, filters and pipe supports) conducting air for the prevention of over- and under-pressure within machinery and marine tanks.

items.name: Machinery and marine tanks over- and under-pressure prevention - watertight integrity upholding
items.definition: Upholding of watertight integrity of air pipes for over- and under-pressure prevention in machinery and marine tanks.
items.commonDefinition: Vent heads mounted on top of air pipes, with the purpose of upholding the watertight integrity of the air pipes and the associated tanks.

items.name: Machinery and marine tanks liquid level determination
items.definition:
Determination of the liquid content of each machinery and marine tank by
- measuring the liquid level in the tank
- measuring the list and the trim of the vessel
- measuring the temperature of the tank content, to determine the density of the content
- calculating the volume and weight of the liquid content based on the measurements and the tank's geometry.
items.commonDefinition:
A system for determination of the liquid content of each machinery and marine tank by
- measuring the liquid level in the tank
- measuring the list and the trim of the vessel
- measuring the temperature of the tank content, to determine the density of the content
- calculating the volume and weight of the liquid content based on the measurements and the tank's geometry.

items.name: Machinery and marine tanks liquid level determination - pipes
items.definition: Inserting a 'sounding tape' into a sounding pipe connected to machinery or marine tanks, to manually determine the liquid level of each tank.
items.commonDefinition:
Piping (i.e. pipes, pipe connections, valves, filters and pipe supports)
- connected to machinery or marine tanks
- for inserting a 'sounding tape' to manually determine the liquid level of each tank.

- measuring the liquid level in the tank
- measuring the list and the trim of the vessel
- measuring the temperature of the tank content, to determine the density of the content
- calculating the volume and weight of the liquid content based on the measurements and the tank's geometry.

A system for remote determination of the liquid content of each machinery and marine tank by

- calculating the volume and weight of the liquid content based on the measurements and the tank's geometry.

Determination of the liquid content of each machinery and marine tank by

- measuring the temperature of the tank content, to determine the density of the content
- calculating the volume and weight of the liquid content based on the measurements and the tank's geometry.

items.name: Exhaust gas handling
items.definition:
Handling of exhaust gas, i.e. gas which occurs as a result of combustion of fuel.
Extraction of heat from the exhaust gases is not covered here, but under e.g. steam generation.
items.commonDefinition:
Systems for handling of exhaust gas, i.e. gas which occurs as a result of combustion of fuel.
Extraction of heat from the exhaust gases is not covered here, but under e.g. steam generation.

items.name: Exhaust gas SOx cleaning
items.definition:
Cleaning of SOx from the exhaust gas before emission to the atmosphere by:
- washing the exhaust gas with sea or fresh water
- control and monitoring of exhaust piping bypass valves
In addition, if not included in the sea or fresh water control and monitoring system, control and monitoring of:
- sea or fresh water pumps supplying the exhaust gas scrubber
- valves in the sea or fresh water piping supplying the exhaust gas scrubber.
items.commonDefinition:
A system for cleaning of SOx from the exhaust gas before emission to the atmosphere by:
- washing the exhaust gas with sea or fresh water
- control and monitoring of exhaust piping bypass valves
In addition, if not included in the sea or fresh water control and monitoring system, control and monitoring of:
- sea or fresh water pumps supplying the exhaust gas scrubber
- valves in the sea or fresh water piping supplying the exhaust gas scrubber.

items.name: Liquefied CO2 containment in independent tanks type C
items.commonName: Liquefied CO2 storage independent tank arrangements type C
items.definition:
Liquefied CO2 containing in independent tanks of type C.
Independent implies that the tanks are self-supporting, do not form part of the vessel's main structure and are not essential to the strength if the vessel's main structure.
Type C (also referred to as pressure vessels) are tanks meeting pressure vessel criteria and having a design vapour pressure as required by IGC Code Ch. 4.2.4.4.
items.commonDefinition:
Arrangements for containment of liquefied CO2 in independent tanks of type C. Independent implies that the tanks are self-supporting, do not form part of the vessel's main structure and are not essential to the strength if the vessel's main structure.
Type C (also referred to as pressure vessels) are tanks meeting pressure vessel criteria and having a design vapour pressure as required by IGC Code Ch. 4.2.4.4.
Each arrangement may include:
- an independent tank, the tank boundaries are the primary barriers of the fuel gas containment system
- thermal insulation
- supports for the independent tank
- a hold space surrounding the independent tank
- supporting structures accommodating loads and moments from the independent tank.

items.name: Transferring loads from the liquefied CO2 independent tank type C to the vessel structure
items.commonName: Liquefied CO2 independent tank type C supports
items.definition: Transfer of loads and moments from a  liquefied CO2 independent tank of type C to the vessel's main structure. The tank should be supported in a manner which will prevent bodily movement of the tank under static and dynamic loads while allowing contraction and expansion of the tank under temperature variations and hull deflections without due stressing of the tank and of the hull.
items.commonDefinition: Supports for transfer of loads and moments from a  liquefied CO2 independent tank of type C to the vessel's main structure. items.name: Object detection by radar systems
items.definition: Object-detection based on the use of radio waves.
items.commonDefinition: Radars are an object-detection systems that use radio waves to determine the range, angle, or velocity of objects. They can be used to detect aircraft, ships, spacecraft, guided missiles, motor vehicles, weather formations, and terrain. A radar system consists of a transmitter producing electromagnetic waves in the radio or microwaves domain, a transmitting antenna, a receiving antenna (often the same antenna is used for transmitting and receiving) and a receiver and processor to determine properties of the object(s). Radio waves (pulsed or continuous) from the transmitter reflect off the object and return to the receiver, giving information about the object's location and speed.

items.name: Object detection by radars, X-band
items.definition: An object-detection system operating in the X-band, i.e. in the frequency range 8.0 to 12.0 GHz
items.commonDefinition: Radars operating in the X-band, i.e. in the frequency range 8.0 to 12.0 GHz. Radar is an object-detection system that uses radio waves to determine the range, angle, or velocity of objects. It can be used to detect aircraft, ships, spacecraft, guided missiles, motor vehicles, weather formations, and terrain. A radar system consists of a transmitter producing electromagnetic waves in the radio or microwaves domain, a transmitting antenna, a receiving antenna (often the same antenna is used for transmitting and receiving) and a receiver and processor to determine properties of the object(s). Radio waves (pulsed or continuous) from the transmitter reflect off the object and return to the receiver, giving information about the object's location and speed.

items.name: Object detection by radars, S-band
items.definition: An object-detection system operating in the S-band, i.e. in the frequency range 2 to 4 GHz
items.commonDefinition: A radar operating in the S-band, i.e. in the frequency range 2 to 4 GHz. Radar is an object-detection system that uses radio waves to determine the range, angle, or velocity of objects. It can be used to detect aircraft, ships, spacecraft, guided missiles, motor vehicles, weather formations, and terrain. A radar system consists of a transmitter producing electromagnetic waves in the radio or microwaves domain, a transmitting antenna, a receiving antenna (often the same antenna is used for transmitting and receiving) and a receiver and processor to determine properties of the object(s). Radio waves (pulsed or continuous) from the transmitter reflect off the object and return to the receiver, giving information about the object's location and speed.

items.name: Grounding avoidance
items.definition: Determining and continuously updating the vessel's position by automatic means, enabling automatic detection of cross-track-error. Evaluation of signals from e.g. electronic chart and information displays (ECDIS), chart radars, speed logs, positioning systems and track control systems.
items.commonDefinition: A system for determining and continuously updating the vessel's position by automatic means, enabling automatic detection of cross-track-error. The system will evaluate signals from e.g. electronic chart and information displays (ECDIS), chart radars, speed logs, positioning systems and track control systems.

items.definition: Use of sound propagation underwater to navigate, communicate with or detect objects on or under the surface of the water.
items.commonDefinition:
Sonar (originally an acronym for Sound Navigation And Ranging) system:
A system that uses sound propagation underwater to navigate, communicate with or detect objects on or under the surface of the water.

- transmitting and receiving general radiocommunications, using either radiotelegraphy or direct-printing telegraphy.
items.commonDefinition:
Satellite communication installations capable of:
- transmitting and receiving distress and safety communications using direct-printing telegraphy
- initiating and receiving distress priority calls
- maintaining watch for shore-to-ship distress alerts, including those directed to specifically defined geographical areas
- transmitting and receiving general radiocommunications, using either radiotelegraphy or direct-printing telegraphy.

An alarm system, which shall be provided to operate from the engine control room or the manoeuvring platform, as appropriate, and shall be clearly audible in the engineers' accommodation. (SOLAS Ch. II-1/38)
The engineers' alarm is normally an integral part of the extension alarm system, but may be a separate system.

Machinery alarms extension: Distribution of machinery alarms to the watch-keeping engineer's cabin, on the navigation bridge and in public spaces.
items.commonDefinition:
Machinery watch responsibility transfer system: A system for selection between manned and unmanned engine room operation, and for activation of the extension alarm system when unmanned operation is selected.
Machinery alarms extension system: A system for distribution of machinery alarms to the watch-keeping engineer's cabin, on the navigation bridge and in public spaces.
Both systems may be integrated in the main alarm system.

items.name: Vessel control and monitoring, integrated
items.definition: Central control and monitoring of more than one function.
items.commonDefinition:
A central control and monitoring system controlling more than one function.
Examples of controlled functions: Electric power generation, ballasting, cargo handling.

items.name: Vessel managing
items.definition: Provision an integrated user interface for safety related systems
items.commonDefinition:
A system supplementary/additional to those required by the rules, interfacing single or multiple control, monitoring, alarm and/or safety systems.
It may provide operational- or decision support in managing e.g.:
- specific vessel- or machinery operations
- emergency- or damage control situations.
The support provided may either be guidance or recommendations to the operator who then decide what actions to effectuate, or automatic actions.
It may contain a variety of functions, links to external systems, and are normally arranged: 
- with dedicated operator stations and servers
- without dedicated I/O, but utilizing data from the interfaced systems through different communication links /network connections.
- safety management system (interfacing relevant systems for fire safety and watertight integrity)
- decision or operational support system (providing overview, analytics and recommendations on e.g. fuel optimization, operational capabilities, automatic operations).

items.name: Monitoring and verifying operation and condition
items.commonName: Operation and condition monitoring and verification systems

items.name: Data collecting, storing, managing and distributing
items.definition: Collection and storage of data from sources onboard. Serving data to onboard consumers and transferring data to remote servers or consumers.
items.commonDefinition: Hardware and software system providing an end to end communication of data from sources onboard a vessel via a vessel server to onboard consumer and via a data relay component to remote data server serving data to remote consumers.

items.name: Data collecting and storing
items.commonDefinition: A processing system (server) for collecting data from on board source systems, storing the data in a buffer and serving them to on board systems for processing or via a data relay component to a remote server.

items.name: Data managing and distributing
items.definition: Managing and distributing collected data, i.e. securing (encrypting, controlling access), quality control, change tracing, monitoring, routing and packaging.

items.name: Emergency shut down
items.definition:
Shut down of hydrocarbon flow. For drilling and production vessels, also removal of ignition sources by shutdown of e.g. electric power generation and other combustion installations. This function only covers the shut down initiation and control, actuating devices as shut down valves are described as a part of the functions they may shut down.
Note: The facilities required for all vessels relating to shut down of ventilation, fuel oil and fuel gas are described as a part of the control and monitoring for the corresponding functions.

items.name: Fire containment
items.definition:
Fire containment in the space of origin by:
- subdivision of the vessel by thermal and structural boundaries
- thermal insulation of the boundaries with due regard to the fire risk of the space and adjacent spaces
- maintaining fire integrity at openings and penetrations.
Control of smoke spread by structural means, e.g. draught stops.
Protection of equipment from heat from a fire by thermal insulation.
items.commonDefinition:
Arrangements for fire containment in the space of origin by:
- subdivision of the vessel by thermal and structural boundaries
- thermal insulation of boundaries with due regard to the fire risk of the space and adjacent spaces
- maintaining fire integrity at openings and penetrations.
Control of smoke spread by structural means, e.g. draught stops.
Protection of equipment from heat from a fire by thermal insulation.

items.name: Fire containment by horizontal boundaries
items.definition:

- subdivision of the vessel by horizontal thermal and structural boundaries
- thermal insulation of the boundaries with due regard to the fire risk of the space and adjacent spaces
- maintaining fire integrity at openings and penetrations.
Control of smoke spread in the vertical direction by structural means, e.g. draught stops.
items.commonDefinition:
Fire containment in the space of origin by:
- subdivision of the vessel by horizontal thermal and structural boundaries
- thermal insulation of the boundaries with due regard to the fire risk of the space and adjacent spaces
- maintaining fire integrity at openings and penetrations.
Control of smoke spread in the vertical direction by structural means, e.g. draught stops.

items.commonDefinition:

items.name: Water spraying
items.definition: Protecting external surfaces or cargo from overheating due to heat radiation form a fire, protecting structural steel against excessive cooling due to liquefied gas leakage or limiting spread of toxic ammonia gas in the event of a leakage by water spraying.
items.commonDefinition: Water spraying systems for to protect external surfaces or cargo from overheating due to heat radiation form a fire, to protect structural steel against excessive cooling due to liquefied gas leakage or limit spread of toxic ammonia gas in the event of a leakage.

items.commonDefinition: Devices for generation and application of fire extinguishing foam as defined in FSS Code 2001 Ch.4.3.2 and SOLAS 2004 Ch.II-2 Reg.7.1.2, 7.2.2 and 7.3.2.

items.name: Fire extinguishing - non-portable / wheeled fire extinguishers
items.definition: Fire extinguishing by devices for application of a fire extinguishing medium that may be transported e.g. by means of wheels, but which is to heavy to be carried. See SOLAS 2004 Ch.II-2 Reg.7.1.3, 7.2.3 and 7.3.1.
items.commonDefinition: Devices for application of a fire extinguishing medium that may be transported e.g. by means of wheels, but which is to heavy to be carried. See SOLAS 2004 Ch.II-2 Reg.7.1.3, 7.2.3 and 7.3.1.

items.name: Fire extinguishing - water mist lances
items.commonDefinition: Lances for penetrating and applying water mist inside standard containers.

items.name: Mustering and safe accommodation
items.definition:
Mustering: Gathering of people in case of an alarm, before proceeding to embarkation.
Safe accommodation: Accommodation of people in an area which can safely accommodate all persons on board in a casualty.
items.commonDefinition:
Muster (assembly) station: A location where people are mustering in case of an alarm, before proceeding to embarkation.
Safe area: An area which can safely accommodate all persons on board in a casualty.

items.name: Means of escape
items.definition:
Provisions providing for persons on board safely and swiftly escape to the lifeboat and liferaft embarkation deck or other safe areas. The provisions shall comprise:
- safe escape routes
- additional aids for escape ensuring accessibility, clear marking and adequate design for emergency situations.
See SOLAS Ch.II-2 Reg.13.
items.commonDefinition:
Routes for walking, providing for persons on board safely and swiftly escape to the lifeboat and liferaft embarkation deck or other safe areas.
See SOLAS Ch.II-2 Reg.13.

items.name: Emergency escape - respiratory and eye protection
items.commonName: Emergency escape respiratory and eye protection equipment

items.name: Emergency escape - breathing air supply
items.definition: Supply of breathing air or oxygen to a person during escape from a compartment that has a hazardous atmosphere.
items.commonDefinition: A device supplying breathing air or oxygen to a person, intended only for escape from a compartment that has a hazardous atmosphere. Ref. FSS Code 2.2.

items.name: Underwater escape
items.definition: Emergency escape from a submerged underwater vehicle or installation.
items.commonDefinition: Arrangements for emergency escape from a submerged underwater vehicle or installation, e.g. rescue vehicles or air trapping devices.

items.name: Survival
items.definition: Emergency escape by means of lifeboats or liferafts.

items.name: Survival - lifeboat arrangements, conventional
items.definition:
Emergency escape by means of conventional lifeboats. The term 'conventional lifeboat' covers all types of lifeboats except free-fall and hyperbaric lifeboats.
This function covers all lifeboat arrangements, each consisting of one conventional lifeboat, stowing equipment, launching and recovery equipment (davits, winch, falls etc.), and embarkation equipment.

items.definition:

This function covers all throw overboard liferaft arrangements on one side, consisting of liferafts, and stowing, launching and embarkation equipment. 
This function shall also be individualised for arrangements located forward or aft (ref. SOLAS III/31.4).

items.definition: Emergency escape by means of buoyant objects that are designed to support a specified number of persons in the water.

items.name: Rescue and recovery
items.definition:
Rescue: Ability to bring persons in distress into a permanent or temporary safe situation.
Recovery: Ability to bring rescued persons on board the vessel.

items.name: Rescue - rescue boat arrangements
items.definition:
Ability to bring persons in distress into a permanent or temporary safe situation by use of rescue boats. A rescue boat may be a dedicated boat or a lifeboat complying with the requirements to a rescue boat.
This function covers all rescue boat arrangements, each consisting of one rescue boat, stowing equipment, and launching and recovery equipment.

items.name: Protection of fire fighting personnel against effects from fires
items.definition:
Protection of fire-fighters against effects from fires, and enabling the fire-fighters to perform this task, i.e.:
- personal protection
- fire-fighting tools
- breathing air supply
- means to find the way back to safety.
items.commonDefinition:
Outfits for use by fire-fighters, each outfit consisting of personal equipment, breathing apparatus and lifeline.
See FSS Code 2.1.

items.name: Protection of personnel against gases and chemicals
items.definition:
Protection of personnel against gases and chemicals, by use of:
- personal equipment
- breathing apparatus
- lifeline.
items.commonDefinition:
Outfits for use by personnel who may come in contact with gases and chemicals, each outfit consisting of personal equipment, breathing apparatus and lifeline.
See IBC Code 14.2.

items.name: Protection of personnel against mechanical hazards
items.definition: Protection of personnel against mechanical hazards by personal equipment.
items.commonDefinition:
Personal equipment for use by crew during work. The type of equipment will vary dependent on the work to be performed, an may consist of:
- clothing, e.g. coverall, boots, gloves, helmet, goggles, ear protection
- tools and lights
- safety devices as Oxygen indicator or gas detector

items.name: Breathing air supply
items.definition: Supply of breathing air to e.g. gas bottles used as a part of fire-fighter's outfits.
items.commonDefinition: Breathing air supply arrangements for e.g. gas bottles used as a part of fire-fighter's outfits.

items.name: Breathing air supply - air compression
items.definition: Compression of air intended for breathing air supply.
items.commonDefinition: Compressor units intended for breathing air supply.

items.name: Breathing air supply - spare air storage
items.definition: Spare capacity of breathing air storage.
items.commonDefinition: Spare gas bottles intended for breathing air.

items.name: Protection of personnel against rotating machinery
Helicopter pick up area
items.definition: A dedicated area for the purpose of lifting objects from the vessel to a helicopter or vice versa.

items.name: Rescue from helicopter deck
items.definition: Rescue of personnel from helicopter deck.
items.commonDefinition: Equipment for rescue in case of accidents on the helicopter deck, consisting of e.g. tools, hooks, ladders, lifelines and fire retardant blankets.

items.name: Ventilating
items.definition:
- Changing or replacing air in any space to provide high indoor air quality. i.e. to control temperature or remove moisture, odours, smoke, heat, dust, airborne bacteria, carbon dioxide, and to replenish oxygen
- supplying air to combustion processes.

items.name: Reciprocating linear piston motion to rotation conversion
items.definition: Convertion of reciprocating linear piston motion into rotation.
items.commonDefinition: The part of an engine that translates reciprocating linear piston motion into rotation. To convert the reciprocating motion into rotation, the crankshaft has "crankthrows" or "crankpins", additional bearing surfaces whose axis is offset from that of the main journal, to which the "big ends" of the connecting rods from each cylinder attach. It typically connects to a flywheel to reduce the pulsation characteristic of the four-stroke cycle, and sometimes a torsional or vibrational damper at the opposite end, to reduce the torsional vibrations often caused along the length of the crankshaft by the cylinders farthest from the output end acting on the torsional elasticity of the metal.

items.name: Gas turbine, multiple shafts
items.definition:
An internal combustion engine generating rotational mechanical power, consisting of a gas generator, a power turbine and auxiliary turbine mounted systems.
- The gas generator consists of an upstream rotating compressor sections mechanically coupled to downstream turbine sections, and a combustor in-between. The high temperature combustion gas enters the turbine, where it expands through the turbine stages, producing rotational mechanical energy driving the compressor.
- The power turbine expands the high temperature combustion gas from the gas generator, where it expands down to the exhaust pressure, producing rotational mechanical energy driving the external mechanical loads.
- The auxiliary turbine mounted systems provides lubrication oil distribution, cooling internally in the turbine, and control and monitoring of the turbine.

items.name: Thermal power generation
items.definition: Generation of thermal power by compression of combustion air, mixing air and fuel, and combustion of mixture.
items.commonDefinition:
A rotating device consisting of a compressor part, a combustor and a turbine part.
- the compressor part sucks air and compresses it before the air enters the combustor
- the combustor is a chamber where the air and the fuel is mixed and combusted before it  enters the turbine part
- the combustion gases expand through the turbine part making the turbine rotate, and the turbine drives the compressor part via a connecting shaft.

items.name: Gas generator containment and flow correction
items.definition:
- compressed combustion air and expanding combustion gas containment
- internal parts and bearings containment and supporting
- flow paths optimising for combustion air comperssor and gas generator turbine
items.commonDefinition:
The assembly conntaining the static parts of the gas generator, i.e. the housing, guide vanes for compressor and gas generator turbine, bleed valves for the compressor and sealings. 
The function of the assembly is:
- containment of compressed combustion air and expanding combustion gases.
- containment and supporting of the internal parts and bearings 
- optimising the air flow for combustion air compression and the combustion gas flow for the gas generator turbine.

items.definition: A hinged frame intended for lifting. Hydraulic cylinders control the movement of the A-frame while a winch is fitted for hoisting and lowering the load.

items.name: Seismic exploration equipment
items.definition:
Equipment for exploration of geological formations below the sea bed using acoustic methods, normally consisting of:
- an air gun on board the vessel providing controlled acoustic pulses transmitted though the water to the geological formations below the sea bed
- hydrophones receiving the acoustic signals reflected from geological formations
- cables positioning the hydrophones and transmitting power to and signals from the hydrophones.

items.name: Steam driven piston engine
items.definition: A reciprocating engine, also known as a piston engine, using one or more reciprocating pistons to convert steam pressure into a rotating motion.

items.name: Sail rig
items.definition: An arrangement where sails supported by e.g. masts, stays or booms transfer the wind forces as propulsion trust to the vessel

items.name: Booms and sails carrying
items.definition: Carrying booms and sails and transferring propulsion thrust to the vessel.
items.commonDefinition:
Vertical spar
- carrying booms and sails
- transferring propulsion thrust to the vessel.

items.name: Sails carrying and adjusting
items.definition: Carrying sails and adjusting the sail angle with respect to the wind.
items.commonDefinition:
Horizontal or inclined booom/spar connected to the mast for
- carrying sails
- adjusting the sail angle with respect to the wind.

items.name: Masts supporting
items.definition: Supporting the mast
items.commonDefinition: Fixed lines, wires, or rods, which support the masts.

items.name: Propulsion thrust generating from wind power
items.definition: Generating thrust from wind power.
items.commonDefinition: A membrane structure made from fabric generating propulsion thrust from wind power.

items.name: Sails deploying, hoisting and lowering
items.definition: Deploying, hoisting and lowering the sails.
items.commonDefinition: Hydraulic and / or mechanical arrangement for deploying and/or, hoisting and lowering the sails.

items.name: Sails trimming
items.definition: Adjusting the profile of the sails to optimise the wind generated propulsion trust.
items.commonDefinition: Hydraulic and / or mechanical arrangements for adjusting the angle and profile of the sails to optimise the wind generated propulsion trust.

items.name: Masts tilting, securing and stowing
items.definition: Tilting, securing and stowing the masts.
items.commonDefinition: Hydraulic and / or mechanical arrangements for tilting, securing and stowing the masts to protect them in heavy weather and/or to reduce the vessel's air draught when passing under bridges or overhead power cables.

items.name: Controlling and monitoring
items.definition: Controlling and monitoring the sail rig, e.g. deployment and trimming of the sails and tilting of the masts.
items.commonDefinition: A system for controlling and monitoring the sail rig, e.g. deployment and trimming of the sails and tilting of the masts.

items.name: Rotor sail
items.definition: A rotating cylindrical sail supported by a mast transferring the lift forces perpendicular to the wind direction ("Magnus effect") generated by the wind passing over the rotating cylindrical sail to propulsion trust for the vessel.

items.name: Sail carrying
items.definition: Carrying the rotating sail and transferring the generated propulsion trust to the vessel.
items.commonDefinition: A spar carrying the rotating sail and transferring the generated propulsion trust to the vessel.

items.name: Propulsion thrust generating from wind power
items.definition: Generating propulsion thrust from wind power.
items.commonDefinition: A rotating fixed cylindrical sail transferring the lift force generated by the wind passing the rotating cylinder to propulsion trust for the vessel.

items.name: Sail rotating
items.definition: Rotating the sail.
items.commonDefinition: An arrangement for rotating the sail.

items.name: Sail driving
items.definition: Converting input energy to rotational mechanical energy for driving the sail.
items.commonDefinition: A device converting input energy to rotational mechanical energy for driving the sail

normalAssignmentNames.C108: Sail rotation steam driven piston engine

items.name: Rotation energy transferring
items.definition: Transferring rotational mechanical energy from the driver to the sail.
items.commonDefinition: A mechanical device transferring rotational mechanical energy from the driver to the sail.

items.name: Sail thrust force uptaking
items.definition:
- enabling rotation between the sail and the inner mast while constraining other relative motion types
- reducing friction between the sail and the inner mast, and
- transferring thust forces from the sail to the inner mast.
items.commonDefinition:
Mechanical devices
- enabling rotation between the sail and the inner mast while constraining other relative motion types
- reducing friction between the sail and the inner mast, and
- transferring thust forces from the sail to the inner mast.

items.name: Tilting, securing and stowing
items.definition: Tilting, securing and stowing the rotor sail.
items.commonDefinition: A hydraulic and / or mechanical arrangement for tilting, securing and stowing the rotor sail to protect it in heavy weather and to reduce the vessel's air draught when passing under bridges or overhead power cables.

items.name: Controlling and monitoring
items.definition: Controlling and monitoring the rotor sail, e.g. rotational speed and direction, tilting and securing.
items.commonDefinition: A system for controlling and monitoring the rotor sail, e.g. rotational speed and direction, tilting and securing.

items.name: Wing rig
items.definition: A wing profiled sail self-supported or supported by a mast transferring the the wind forces to propulsion thrust for the vessel.

items.name: Sail carrying
items.definition: Carrying the wing sail and transferring the generated thrust to the vessel.
items.commonDefinition: A mast carrying the wing sail and transferring the generated thrust to the vessel.

items.name: Propulsion thrust generating from wind power
items.definition: Generating thrust from wind power.
items.commonDefinition: A fixed symetric or adjustable wing profiled sail made from fabric or rigid materials self-supported or supported by webs or ribs, transferring the wind forces to propulsion trust to the vessel.

items.name: Wing sail deploying, hoisting and lowering
items.definition: Deploying, hoisting and lowering the wing rig.
items.commonDefinition: Hydraulic and / or mechanical arrangement for deploying, hoisting and lowering the wing rig.

items.name: Wing sail trimming
items.definition: Adjusting the profile of the wing sail to optimise the wind generated propulsion thrust.
items.commonDefinition: A hydraulic and / or mechanical arrangement for adjusting the angle and/or profile of the wing sail to optimise the wind generated propulsion thrust.

items.name: Wing rig tilting, securing and stowing
items.definition: Tilting, securing and stowing the mast.
items.commonDefinition: A hydraulic and / or mechanical arrangement for tilting, securing and stowing the mast to protect it in heavy weather and to reduce the vessel's air draught when passing under bridges or overhead power cables.

items.name: Controlling and monitoring
items.definition: Controlling and monitoring the wing rig, e.g. wing angle against the wind, wing profile, tilting and securing.
items.commonDefinition: A system for controlling and monitoring the wing rig, e.g. wing angle against the wind, wing profile, tilting and securing.

items.name: Ventilated foil
items.definition: A wing profiled foil self-supported or supported by a mast transferring the the wind forces to propulsion thrust for the vessel. The profile is fitted with a tail at the aft windward side that together with a fan creatin an underpressuer inside the profile and perforations at the aft leeward side of the profile to optimise the air flow araound the profile and therby maximise the lift force.

items.name: Sail carrying
items.definition: Carrying the wing sail and transferring the generated trust to the vessel.
items.commonDefinition: A mast carrying the ventilated foil  and transferring the generated trust to the vessel.

items.name: Propulsion thrust generating from wind power
items.definition: Generating thrust from wind power.
items.commonDefinition: A fixed symetric wing profiled foil made from fabric or rigid materials self-supported or supported by webs or ribs, transferring the wind forces to propulsion trust to the mast or directly to the vessel.

items.name: Wing sail deploying, hoisting and lowering
items.definition: Deploying, hoisting and lowering the ventilated foil.
items.commonDefinition: Hydraulic and / or mechanical arrangement for deploying, hoisting and lowering the ventilated foil.

items.name: Foil trimming
items.definition: Adjusting the profile of the wing sail to optimise the wind generated propulsion thrust.
items.commonDefinition: A hydraulic and / or mechanical arrangement for adjusting the angle and position the tail of the ventilated foil to optimise the wind generated propulsion thrust.

items.name: Foil ventilating
items.definition: Vacuum generting inside the foil profile to optimise the air flow araound the profile and therby maximise the lift force by use of a fan.
items.commonDefinition: A fan generatin a vacuum inside the foil profile to optimise the air flow araound the profile and therby maximise the lift force.

items.name: Vantilated foil tilting, securing and stowing
items.definition: Tilting, securing and stowing the mast.
items.commonDefinition: A hydraulic and / or mechanical arrangement for tilting, securing and stowing the mast to protect it in heavy weather and to reduce the vessel's air draught when passing under bridges or overhead power cables.

items.name: Controlling and monitoring
items.definition: Controlling and monitoring the ventilated foil, e.g. foil angle against the wind, position of tail, fan, deploying, tilting and securing.
items.commonDefinition: A system for controlling and monitoring the ventilated foil , e.g. the  profile angle against the wind, position of tail, fan, deployment, tilting and securing.

items.name: Gas turbine, single shaft
items.definition:
An internal combustion engine generating rotational mechanical power, consisting of a compressor, a combustor, a turbine and auxiliary turbine mounted systems.
- the compressor sucks air and compresses it before the air enters the combustor
- the combustor is a chamber where the air and the fuel is mixed and combusted before it enters the turbine part
- the combustion gases expand through the turbine making the turbine rotate, and the turbine drives the compressor and the devices driven by the turbine.
- the auxiliary turbine mounted systems provide lubrication oil distribution, and control and monitoring of the turbine.

items.name: Rotational mechanical power generation
items.definition: Conversion of chemical power to rotational mechanical power by a combustion process.
items.commonDefinition:
A rotating device generating rotational mechanical power, consisting of a compressor, a combustor and a turbine.
- the compressor part sucks air and compresses it before the air enters the combustor
- the combustor is a chamber where the air and the fuel is mixed and combusted before it enters the turbine part
- the combustion gases expand through the turbine part making the turbine rotate, and the turbine drives the compressor part via a connecting shaft.

items.name: Turbine containment
items.commonDefinition:
A mechanical housing including guide vanes and nozzles providing:
- containment of air, fuel and gases resulting from combustion
- supporting of the internal parts and bearings
- creating correct flow paths.

items.name: Combustion gases containment
items.definition: Containment of air, fuel and gases resulting from combustion, supporting of the internal parts and creating correct flow paths.
items.commonDefinition:
A mechanical housing including guide vanes and nozzles providing:
- containment of air, fuel and gases resulting from combustion
- supporting of the internal parts and bearings
- creating correct flow paths.

items.name: Compressor stabilisation
items.definition: Control and stabilisation of the compressor's operational characteristic in transient conditions.
items.commonDefinition: A valve for control and statbilisation of the compressor's operational characteristic in transient conditions.

items.name: Leakage prevention
items.definition: Preventing leakage of gases and lubrication oil internally and externally, and preventing mixing of gases and lubrication oil.
items.commonDefinition: Seals preventing leakage of gases and lubrication oil internally and externally, and preventing mixing of gases and lubrication oil.

items.name: Turbine rotating parts assembly
items.definition: Enabling rotation of the turbine.
items.commonDefinition: An arrangement enabling rotation of the turbine, consisting of shaft, tie rods, radial bearings and thrust bearings.

items.name: Rotating parts support
items.definition: Connecting the compressor with the turbine, and supporting all rotating parts.
items.commonDefinition: A shaft connecting the compressor with the turbine, while supporting all rotating parts.

items.name: Rotating parts fastening
items.definition: Fastening the parts of the shaft arrangement to each other.
items.commonDefinition: Bolts fastening the parts of the shaft arrangement to each other.

items.name: Turbine shaft rotation enabling
items.definition:
- enabling rotation between the turbine shaft and the turbine while constraining other relative motion types
- reducing friction between the turbine shaft and the turbine, and
- transferring radial forces between the turbine shaft and the turbine.
items.commonDefinition:
Mechanical devices
- enabling rotation between the turbine shaft and the turbine while constraining other relative motion types
- reducing friction between the turbine shaft and the turbine, and
- transferring radial forces between the turbine shaft and the turbine.

items.name: Turbine shaft thrust force uptaking

- enabling rotation between the turbine shaft and the turbine while constraining other relative motion types
- reducing friction between the turbine shaft and the turbine, and
- transferring thust forces from the turbine shaft to the turbine.
items.commonDefinition:
Mechanical devices
- enabling rotation between the turbine shaft and the turbine while constraining other relative motion types
- reducing friction between the turbine shaft and the turbine, and
- transferring thust forces from the turbine shaft to the turbine.

items.commonDefinition:
A rotating arrangement for compression of combustion air, constructed by
- impellers, or
- bladed disks.

items.name: Conversion of thermal power to mechanical power
items.definition: Conversion of thermal power to mechanical power for the purpose of driving the compressor.
items.commonDefinition: A turbine converting thermal energy to mechanical energy for the purpose of driving the compressor.

items.commonDefinition:
An arrangement for adding fuel to the compressed air and creating a combustion of the mixture, consisting of:
- a nozzle for vapourising of the fuel and mixing it with the air
- an igniter
- flame tubes where the combustion process takes place.

items.name: Starting
items.definition: Starting of the gas turbine.

items.name: Lubrication

items.name: Internal cooling
items.definition: Cooling by air of internal parts of the gas turbine.
items.commonDefinition: A system for air cooling of internal parts of the gas turbine.

items.name: Controlling and monitoring
items.definition:
Controlling and monitoring the gas turbine, including:
- monitoring of selected parameters, e.g. temperature, pressure, rotational speed
- control of turbine operation, e.g. speed governing
- control of fuel supply
- safety related functions including automatic shutdown.
items.commonDefinition:
Systems for control and monitoring of the gas turbine, including:
- monitoring of selected parameters, e.g. temperature, pressure, rotational speed
- control of turbine operation, e.g. speed governing
- control of fuel supply
- safety related functions including automatic shutdown.

Including pipe connections for drilling fluid inlet and outlet, and for discharge of sand. Including agitarors used within the tank. Including tank mounted monitoring devices, e.g. level indicators.

items.name: Thruster, cycloidal
items.definition:
Thruster: A device for conversion of rotational power to thrust.
A cycloidal thruster consists of a stationary part and a horizontal rotating part with vertical propeller blades along its periphery. The amount and direction of the thrust is controlled by the pitch of the propeller blades.
